revision.comment: Auto-generated page using script written by Immanuelle, either as a placeholder redirect or to make bots recognize the page as made by Immanuelle
revision.text: #redirect[[en:Okutsuki]] [[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects]]

'''奥都墓'''（おくつき）とは、上代の墓のこと。またそこから[[神道]]式の[[墓]]のこと。神道式の[[墓石]]に刻まれる文字でもある。'''奥津墓'''、'''奥城'''とも書く。
「都・津（つ）」は、上代の[[格助詞]]「つ」に当てた[[万葉仮名]]で、現在の「の」と同じ意味がある。。「都」は、神官、氏子などの仕事に従事した人の墓に使われる漢字で、「津」は一般の信徒の墓に使われる。ただし、先祖に[[神官]]、[[氏子]]の[[役]]に従事していた人がいる場合、「都」が使われることがある。またこれとは別に、地域により、どちらかの文字が広く用いられることもあり、一概ではない。
「奥（おく）」とは、奥深い意の「奥」や「置く」を意味するといわれる。「城（き）」は棚・壁などで四辺を取り囲んだ一郭の場所をいい、「[[柩]]（ひつぎ）」の意味もあるとされる。全体の意味としては、「奥深い所にあって外部から遮られた境域」ということであり、また「柩を置く場所」の意となる。
==神道墓== 基本的な構成は、仏式と同じだが、神道では[[焼香]]を行わないので、[[香炉]]は必要ない。また[[玉串]]を奉げる為の[[八足台]]が必要となる。 墓石の形は、細長い[[角柱]]型で、頂上部は[[四角錐]]になっている。この形は[[三種の神器]]の一つ[[天叢雲剣]]を表しているとされる。
墓石には「○○家之奥都城」と刻む。墓石がない場合は墓標に「○○大人（刀自）命之奥都城」のように書く。神道では、[[戒名]]はなく[[霊名]]を用いる。霊名は姓名の下に之霊、命、命霊、霊位、大人、刀自などを付ける。
[[神社]]では通常[[墓地]]を所有していない。神式でお墓を建之する場合は公営や民営の[[霊園]]の[[墓地]]を購入する必要がある。
[[Category:墓|おくつき]]

revision.comment: ページ [[奥津城]] を [[奥都城]] へ移動

==神道墓== 基本的な構成は、仏式と同じだが、神道では[[焼香]]を行わないので、[[香炉]]は必要ない。また[[玉串]]を奉げる為の[[八足台]]が必要となる。 墓石の形は、細長い[[角柱]]型で、頂上部は[[四角錐]]になっている。この形は[[三種の神器]]の一つ[[天叢雲剣]]を表しているとされる。
墓石には「○○家之奥都城」と刻む。墓石がない場合は墓標に「○○大人（刀自）命之奥都城」のように書く。神道では、[[戒名]]はなく、姓名の下に、之霊・命・命霊・霊位などを付ける。
[[神社]]では通常[[墓地]]を所有していない。神式でお墓を建之する場合は公営や民営の[[霊園]]の[[墓地]]を購入する必要がある。
==関連項目== *[[御霊舎]]
[[Category:墓|おくつき]] [[category:神道|おくつき]]

'''奥都城'''（おくつき）とは、上代の墓のこと。またそこから[[神道]]式の[[墓]]のこと。神道式の[[墓石]]に刻まれる文字でもある。'''奥津城'''、'''奥城'''とも書く。
「都・津（つ）」は、上代の[[格助詞]]「つ」に当てた[[万葉仮名]]で、「～の」の意味になる。「都」は、神官・氏子などを勤めた人の墓に使われる漢字で、「津」は一般信徒の墓に使われる。ただし、先祖に[[神官]]、[[氏子]]の[[役]]に従事した人がいる場合には「都」が使われることがある。またこれとは別に、地域により、どちらかの文字が広く用いられることもあり、一概ではない。

==神道墓== 基本的な構成は、仏式と同じだが、神道では[[焼香]]を行わないので、[[香炉]]は必要ない。また[[玉串]]を奉げる為の[[八足台]]が必要となる。 墓石の形は、細長い[[角柱]]型で、頂上部は[[四角錐]]になっている。この形は[[三種の神器]]の一つ[[天叢雲剣]]を表しているとされる。
墓石には「○○家之奥都城」と刻む。墓石がない場合は墓標に「○○大人（刀自）命之奥都城」のように書く。神道では[[戒名]]はなく、姓名の下に、之霊・命・命霊・霊位などを付ける。
[[神社]]では通常[[墓地]]を所有していない。神式でお墓を建之する場合は公営や民営の[[霊園]]の[[墓地]]を購入する必要がある。
==関連項目== *[[御霊舎]]

{{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おくつき}} [[Category:墓|おくつき]] [[category:神道|おくつき]]

{{神道}} '''奥都城'''（おくつき）とは、上代の墓のこと。またそこから[[神道]]式の[[墓]]のこと。神道式の[[墓石]]に刻まれる文字でもある。'''奥津城'''、'''奥城'''とも書く。
「都・津（つ）」は、上代の[[格助詞]]「つ」に当てた[[万葉仮名]]で、「～の」の意味になる。「都」は、神官・氏子などを勤めた人の墓に使われる漢字で、「津」は一般信徒の墓に使われる。ただし、先祖に[[神官]]、[[氏子]]の[[役]]に従事した人がいる場合には「都」が使われることがある。またこれとは別に、地域により、どちらかの文字が広く用いられることもあり、一概ではない。
「奥（おく）」とは、奥深い意の「奥」や「置く」を意味するといわれる。「城（き）」は、古代の「[[胆沢城]]」の「城」の用例にみるように棚・壁などで四辺を取り囲んだ一郭の場所をいい、また「[[柩]]（ひつぎ）」の意味もあるとされる。全体の意味としては、「奥深い所にあって外部から遮られた境域」ということであり、また「柩を置く場所」の意となる。
文献上の例として、『[[万葉集]]』に「奥都城」、『[[日本書紀]]』神代巻に「奥津棄戸（おくつ すたへ）」と記されている。本来の意は、死体遺棄による葬法を表しているものであり<ref>藤井正雄 『仏事の基礎知識』 講談社 初版1985年 ISBN 4-06-201068-2 p.116参考</ref>、一般民衆の死体が遺棄されていた事による（考古学上においても、古代日本において一般人は墓を築いた形跡はなく、遺棄された状態である）。『[[伊呂波字類抄]]』、『[[秦山集]]』、『[[伊勢物語]]』、『[[古事記伝]]』などの文献中に葬式の事を「はふる」と記しているが、これも遺棄を意味するものであり、奥都城と同様の意味であるとされる<!-- 藤井正雄 『仏事の基礎知識』p.115 -->（『古事記伝』での表記は「波夫里（はふり）」と記す）。神道墓の形成は、後世の神道家によって大成されたものである。
==神道墓== 基本的な構成は、仏式と同じだが、神道では[[焼香]]を行わないので、[[香炉]]は必要ない。また[[玉串]]を奉げる為の[[八足台]]が必要となる。 墓石の形は、細長い[[角柱]]型で、頂上部は[[四角錐]]になっている。この形は[[三種の神器]]の一つ[[天叢雲剣]]を表しているとされる。
墓石には「○○家之奥都城」と刻む。墓石がない場合は墓標に「○○大人（刀自）命之奥都城」のように書く。神道では[[戒名]]はなく、姓名の下に、之霊・命・命霊・霊位などを付ける。
[[神社]]では通常[[墓地]]を所有していない。神式でお墓を建之する場合は公営や民営の[[霊園]]の[[墓地]]を購入する必要がある。
日本軍人の墓は神道墓で建てられていることが多い。

文献上の例として、『[[万葉集]]』に「奥都城」、『[[日本書紀]]』神代巻に「奥津棄戸（おくつ すたへ）」と記されている。本来の意は、死体遺棄による葬法を表しているものであり<ref>藤井正雄 『仏事の基礎知識』 講談社 初版1985年 ISBN 4-06-201068-2 p.116参考</ref>、一般民衆の死体が遺棄されていた事による（考古学上においても、古代日本において一般人は墓を築いた形跡はなく、遺棄された状態である<ref>『[[吾妻鏡]]』[[弘長]]元年（[[1261年]]）2月29日条には、幕府が関東諸侯において、「～死屍（しし）を路地に弃（す）つる事を禁制すべし」と定めている事からも、死体遺棄が中世前半の庶民にとっても一般的な行いであった事が分かる。</ref>）。『[[伊呂波字類抄]]』、『[[秦山集]]』、『[[伊勢物語]]』、『[[古事記伝]]』などの文献中に葬式の事を「はふる」と記しているが、これも遺棄を意味するものであり、奥都城と同様の意味であるとされる<!-- 藤井正雄 『仏事の基礎知識』p.115 -->（『古事記伝』での表記は「波夫里（はふり）」と記す）。神道墓の形成は、後世の神道家によって大成されたものである。

{{神道}} '''奥都城'''（おくつき）とは、[[上代]]の[[墓]]のこと。またそこから[[神道]]式の墓のこと。神道式の[[墓石]]に刻まれる文字でもある。'''奥津城'''、'''奥城'''とも書く。
「都・津（つ）」は、上代の[[格助詞]]「つ」に当てた[[万葉仮名]]で、「～の」の意味になる。「都」は、神官・氏子などを勤めた人の墓に使われる漢字で、「津」は一般信徒の墓に使われる。ただし、先祖に[[神官]]、[[氏子]]の[[役]]に従事した人がいる場合には「都」が使われることがある。またこれとは別に、地域により、どちらかの文字が広く用いられることもあり、一概ではない。
「奥（おく）」とは、奥深い意の「奥」や「置く」を意味するといわれる。「城（き）」は、古代の「[[胆沢城]]」の「城」の用例にみるように棚・壁などで四辺を取り囲んだ一郭の場所をいい、また「[[柩]]（ひつぎ）」の意味もあるとされる。全体の意味としては、「奥深い所にあって外部から遮られた境域」ということであり、また「柩を置く場所」の意となる。
文献上の例として、『[[万葉集]]』に「奥都城」、『[[日本書紀]]』神代巻に「奥津棄戸（おくつ すたへ）」と記されている。本来の意は、死体遺棄による葬法を表しているものであり<ref>藤井正雄 『仏事の基礎知識』 講談社 初版1985年 ISBN 4-06-201068-2 p.116参考</ref>、一般民衆の死体が遺棄されていた事による（考古学上においても、古代日本において一般人は墓を築いた形跡はなく、遺棄された状態である<ref>『[[吾妻鏡]]』[[弘長]]元年（[[1261年]]）2月29日条には、幕府が関東諸侯において、「～死屍（しし）を路地に弃（す）つる事を禁制すべし」と定めている事からも、死体遺棄が中世前半の庶民にとっても一般的な行いであった事が分かる。</ref>）。『[[伊呂波字類抄]]』、『[[秦山集]]』、『[[伊勢物語]]』、『[[古事記伝]]』などの文献中に葬式の事を「はふる」と記しているが、これも遺棄を意味するものであり、奥都城と同様の意味であるとされる<!-- 藤井正雄 『仏事の基礎知識』p.115 -->（『古事記伝』での表記は「波夫里（はふり）」と記す）。神道墓の形成は、後世の神道家によって大成されたものである。
==神道墓== 基本的な構成は、仏式と同じだが、神道では[[焼香]]を行わないので、[[香炉]]は必要ない。また[[玉串]]を奉げる為の[[八足台]]が必要となる。 墓石の形は、細長い[[角柱]]型で、頂上部は[[四角錐]]になっている。この形は[[三種の神器]]の一つ[[天叢雲剣]]を表しているとされる。
墓石には「○○家之奥都城」と刻む。墓石がない場合は墓標に「○○大人（刀自）命之奥都城」のように書く。神道では[[戒名]]はなく、姓名の下に、之霊・命・命霊・霊位などを付ける。
[[神社]]では通常[[墓地]]を所有していない。神式でお墓を建之する場合は公営や民営の[[霊園]]の[[墓地]]を購入する必要がある。
日本軍人の墓は神道墓で建てられていることが多い。

文献上の例として、『[[万葉集]]』に「奥都城」、『[[日本書紀]]』神代巻に「奥津棄戸（おくつ すたへ）」と記されている。本来の意は、死体遺棄による葬法を表しているものであり<ref>藤井正雄 『仏事の基礎知識』 講談社 初版1985年 ISBN 4-06-201068-2 p.116参考</ref>、一般民衆の死体が遺棄されていた事による（考古学上においても、古代日本において一般人は墓を築いた形跡はなく、遺棄された状態である<ref>『[[吾妻鏡]]』[[弘長]]元年（[[1261年]]）2月29日条には、幕府が関東諸侯において、「～死屍（しし）を路地に弃（す）つる事を禁制すべし」と定めている事からも、死体遺棄が中世前半の庶民にとっても一般的な行いであった事が分かる。</ref>）。『[[伊呂波字類抄]]』、『[[秦山集]]』、『[[伊勢物語]]』、『[[古事記伝]]』などの文献中に葬式の事を「はふる」と記しているが、これも遺棄を意味するものであり、奥都城と同様の意味であるとされる<!-- 藤井正雄 『仏事の基礎知識』p.115 -->（『古事記伝』での表記は「波夫里（はふり）」と記す）。神道墓の形成は、後世の神道家によって大成されたものである。
==神道墓== 基本的な構成は、仏式と同じであるが、神道では[[焼香]]を行わないので、[[香炉]]は要らない。また[[玉串]]を奉げる為の[[八足台]]が要る。 墓石の形は、細長い[[角柱]]型で、頂上部は[[四角錐]]になっている。この形は[[三種の神器]]の一つ[[天叢雲剣]]を表しているとされる。
墓石には「○○家奥都城」或いは「○○家奥津城」と刻む。墓石がない場合は墓標に「○○大人（刀自）命奥都城」と書く。神道では[[戒名]]はなく、姓名の下に、之霊・命・命霊・霊位などを付ける。
[[神社]]では通常[[墓地]]を所有していない。神式でお墓を建之する場合は公営や民営の[[霊園]]の[[墓地]]を買わなければならない。
日本軍人の墓は神道墓で建てられていることが多い。
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
==関連項目== *[[御霊舎]] *[[生祀]] *[[神葬祭]]

revision.text: {{神道}} '''奥都城'''（おくつき）とは、[[上代]]の[[墓]]のこと。またそこから[[神道]]式の墓のこと。神道式の[[墓石]]に刻まれる文字でもある。'''奥津城'''、'''奥城'''とも書く。 「都・津（つ）」は、上代の[[格助詞]]「つ」に当てた[[万葉仮名]]で、「～の」の意味になる。「都」は、神官・氏子などを勤めた人の墓に使われる漢字で、「津」は一般信徒の墓に使われる。ただし、先祖に[[神官]]、[[氏子]]の[[役]]に従事した人がいる場合には「都」が使われることがある。またこれとは別に、地域により、どちらかの文字が広く用いられることもあり、一概ではない。 「奥（おく）」とは、奥深い意の「奥」や「置く」を意味するといわれる。「城（き）」は、古代の「[[胆沢城]]」の「城」の用例にみるように棚・壁などで四辺を取り囲んだ一郭の場所をいい、また「[[柩]]（ひつぎ）」の意味もあるとされる。全体の意味としては、「奥深い所にあって外部から遮られた境域」ということであり、また「柩を置く場所」の意となる。 文献上の例として、『[[万葉集]]』に「奥都城」、『[[日本書紀]]』神代巻に「奥津棄戸（おくつ すたへ）」と記されている。本来の意は、死体遺棄による葬法を表しているものであり藤井正雄 『仏事の基礎知識』 講談社 初版1985年 ISBN 4-06-201068-2 p.116参考、一般民衆の死体が遺棄されていた事による（考古学上においても、古代日本において一般人は墓を築いた形跡はなく、遺棄された状態である『[[吾妻鏡]]』[[弘長]]元年（[[1261年]]）2月29日条には、幕府が関東諸侯において、「～死屍（しし）を路地に弃（す）つる事を禁制すべし」と定めている事からも、死体遺棄が中世前半の庶民にとっても一般的な行いであった事が分かる。）。『[[伊呂波字類抄]]』、『[[秦山集]]』、『[[伊勢物語]]』、『[[古事記伝]]』などの文献中に葬式の事を「はふる」と記しているが、これも遺棄を意味するものであり、奥都城と同様の意味であるとされる（『古事記伝』での表記は「波夫里（はふり）」と記す）。神道墓の形成は、後世の神道家によって大成されたものである。 ==神道墓== 基本的な構成は、仏式と同じであるが、神道では[[焼香]]を行わないので、[[香炉]]は要らない。また[[玉串]]を奉げる為の[[八足台]]が要る。 墓石の形は、細長い[[角柱]]型で、頂上部は[[四角錐]]になっている。この形は[[三種の神器]]の一つ[[天叢雲剣]]を表しているとされる。 墓石には「○○家奥都城」或いは「○○家奥津城」と刻む。墓石がない場合は墓標に「○○大人（刀自）命奥都城」と書く。神道では[[戒名]]はなく、姓名の下に、之霊・命・命霊・霊位などを付ける。 [[神社]]では通常[[墓地]]を所有していない。神式でお墓を建之する場合は公営や民営の[[霊園]]の[[墓地]]を買わなければならない。 日本軍人の墓は神道墓で建てられていることが多い。[[[Wikipedia:「要出典」をクリックされた方へ|要出典]]] == 脚注 == {{reflist}} ==関連項目== *[[御霊舎]] *[[生祀]] *[[神葬祭]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おくつき}} [[Category:墓|おくつき]] [[category:神道|おくつき]]

{{神道}} '''奥都城'''（おくつき）とは、[[上代]]の[[墓]]のこと。またそこから[[神道]]式の墓のこと。神道式の[[墓石]]に刻まれる文字でもある。'''奥津城'''、'''奥城'''とも書く。

「奥（おく）」とは、奥深い意の「奥」や「置く」を意味するといわれる。「城（き）」は、古代の「[[胆沢城]]」の「城」の用例にみるように棚・壁などで四辺を取り囲んだ一郭の場所をいい、また「[[柩]]（ひつぎ）」の意味もあるとされる。全体の意味としては、「奥深い所にあって外部から遮られた境域」ということであり、また「柩を置く場所」の意となる。
文献上の例として、『[[万葉集]]』に「奥都城」、『[[日本書紀]]』神代巻に「奥津棄戸（おくつ すたへ）」と記されている。本来の意は、死体遺棄による葬法を表しているものであり<ref>藤井正雄 『仏事の基礎知識』 講談社 初版1985年 ISBN 4-06-201068-2 p.116参考</ref>、一般民衆の死体が遺棄されていた事による（考古学上においても、古代日本において一般人は墓を築いた形跡はなく、遺棄された状態である<ref>『[[吾妻鏡]]』[[弘長]]元年（[[1261年]]）2月29日条には、幕府が関東諸侯において、「～死屍（しし）を路地に弃（す）つる事を禁制すべし」と定めている事からも、死体遺棄が中世前半の庶民にとっても一般的な行いであった事が分かる。</ref>）。『[[伊呂波字類抄]]』、『[[秦山集]]』、『[[伊勢物語]]』、『[[古事記伝]]』などの文献中に葬式の事を「はふる」と記しているが、これも遺棄を意味するものであり、奥都城と同様の意味であるとされる<!-- 藤井正雄 『仏事の基礎知識』p.115 -->（『古事記伝』での表記は「波夫里（はふり）」と記す）。神道墓の形成は、後世の神道家によって大成されたものである。
== 神道墓 == 基本的な構成は、仏式と同じであるが、神道では[[焼香]]を行わないので、[[香炉]]は要らない。また[[玉串]]を奉げる為の[[八足台]]が要る。 墓石の形は、細長い[[角柱]]型で、頂上部は[[四角錐]]になっている。この形は[[三種の神器]]の一つ[[天叢雲剣]]を表しているとされる。
墓石には「○○家奥都城」或いは「○○家奥津城」と刻む。墓石がない場合は墓標に「○○大人（刀自）命奥都城」と書く。神道では[[戒名]]はなく、姓名の下に、之霊・命・命霊・霊位などを付ける。
[[神社]]では通常[[墓地]]を所有していない。神式でお墓を建立する場合は公営や民営の[[霊園]]の[[墓地]]を買わなければならない。
日本軍人の墓は神道墓で建てられていることが多い。{{要出典|date=2014年8月}}

{{神道}} '''奥都城'''（おくつき）とは、[[上代]]の[[墓]]のこと。またそこから[[神道]]式の墓のこと。神道式の[[墓石]]に刻まれる文字でもある。'''奥津城'''、'''奥城'''とも書く。
== 言葉の意味 == 「都・津（つ）」は、上代の[[格助詞]]「つ」に当てた[[万葉仮名]]で、「～の」の意味になる。「都」は、神官・氏子などを勤めた人の墓に使われる漢字で、「津」は一般信徒の墓に使われる。ただし、先祖に[[神官]]、[[氏子]]の[[役]]に従事した人がいる場合には「都」が使われることがある。またこれとは別に、地域により、どちらかの文字が広く用いられることもあり、一概ではない。
「奥（おく）」とは、奥深い意の「奥」や「置く」を意味するといわれる。「城（き）」は、古代の「[[胆沢城]]」の「城」の用例にみるように棚・壁などで四辺を取り囲んだ一郭の場所をいい、また「[[柩]]（ひつぎ）」の意味もあるとされる。全体の意味としては、「奥深い所にあって外部から遮られた境域」ということであり、また「柩を置く場所」の意となる。
文献上の例として、『[[万葉集]]』に「奥都城」、『[[日本書紀]]』神代巻に「奥津棄戸（おくつ すたへ）」と記されている。本来の意は、死体遺棄による葬法を表しているものであり<ref>藤井正雄 『仏事の基礎知識』 講談社 初版1985年 ISBN 4-06-201068-2 p.116参考</ref>、一般民衆の死体が遺棄されていた事による（考古学上においても、古代日本において一般人は墓を築いた形跡はなく、遺棄された状態である<ref>『[[吾妻鏡]]』[[弘長]]元年（[[1261年]]）2月29日条には、幕府が関東諸侯において、「～死屍（しし）を路地に弃（す）つる事を禁制すべし」と定めている事からも、死体遺棄が中世前半の庶民にとっても一般的な行いであった事が分かる。</ref>）。『[[伊呂波字類抄]]』、『[[秦山集]]』、『[[伊勢物語]]』、『[[古事記伝]]』などの文献中に葬式の事を「はふる」と記しているが、これも遺棄を意味するものであり、奥都城と同様の意味であるとされる<!-- 藤井正雄 『仏事の基礎知識』p.115 -->（『古事記伝』での表記は「波夫里（はふり）」と記す）。神道墓の形成は、後世の神道家によって大成されたものである。なお、[[縄文時代]]に[[貝塚]]に遺骸を捨てる風習があるが、これは一説<!-- 河野広道説 参考は後述書 p.132. -->に、貝塚自体が全ての生物の霊魂を他界へ送り返すための祭の場であり<!-- 河野説 参考後述書 -->、単なるゴミ捨て場ではなく、埋葬場として用いられたとするものがある<ref>[[渡辺誠]] 『縄文時代の知識 考古学シリーズ4』 [[東京美術]] ISBN 4-1087-0190-1 1983年 p.132.[[河野広道]]が[[アイヌ]]の習俗をヒントに得た説で、このため、親子兄弟の遺骸を棄てたとする。</ref>。
== 神道墓 == 基本的な構成は、仏式と同じであるが、神道では[[焼香]]を行わないので、[[香炉]]は要らない。また[[玉串]]を奉げる為の[[八足台]]が要る。 墓石の形は、細長い[[角柱]]型で、頂上部は[[四角錐]]になっている。この形は[[三種の神器]]の一つ[[天叢雲剣]]を表しているとされる。
墓石には「○○家奥都城」或いは「○○家奥津城」と刻む。墓石がない場合は墓標に「○○大人（刀自）命奥都城」と書く。神道では[[戒名]]はなく、姓名の下に、之霊・命・命霊・霊位などを付ける。
[[神社]]では通常[[墓地]]を所有していない。神式でお墓を建立する場合は公営や民営の[[霊園]]の[[墓地]]を買わなければならない。
日本軍人の墓は神道墓で建てられていることが多い。{{要出典|date=2014年8月}}

== 言葉の意味 == 「都・津（つ）」は、上代の[[格助詞]]「つ」に当てた[[万葉仮名]]で、「～の」の意味になる。「都」は、神官・氏子などを勤めた人の墓に使われる漢字で、「津」は一般信徒の墓に使われる。ただし、先祖に[[神官]]、[[氏子]]の[[役]]に従事した人がいる場合には「都」が使われることがある。またこれとは別に、地域により、どちらかの文字が広く用いられることもあり、一概ではない。
「奥（おく）」とは、奥深い意の「奥」や「置く」を意味するといわれる。「城（き）」は、古代の「[[胆沢城]]」の「城」の用例にみるように棚・壁などで四辺を取り囲んだ一郭の場所をいい、また「[[柩]]（ひつぎ）」の意味もあるとされる。全体の意味としては、「奥深い所にあって外部から遮られた境域」ということであり、また「柩を置く場所」の意となる。
文献上の例として、『[[万葉集]]』に「奥都城」、『[[日本書紀]]』神代巻に「奥津棄戸（おくつ すたへ）」と記されている。本来の意は、死体遺棄による葬法を表しているものであり<ref>藤井正雄 『仏事の基礎知識』 講談社 初版1985年 ISBN 4-06-201068-2 p.116参考</ref>、一般民衆の死体が遺棄されていた事による（考古学上においても、古代日本において一般人は墓を築いた形跡はなく、遺棄された状態である<ref>『[[吾妻鏡]]』[[弘長]]元年（[[1261年]]）2月29日条には、幕府が関東諸侯において、「～死屍（しし）を路地に弃（す）つる事を禁制すべし」と定めている事からも、死体遺棄が中世前半の庶民にとっても一般的な行いであった事が分かる。</ref>）。『[[伊呂波字類抄]]』、『[[秦山集]]』、『[[伊勢物語]]』、『[[古事記伝]]』などの文献中に葬式の事を「はふる」と記しているが、これも遺棄を意味するものであり、奥都城と同様の意味であるとされる<!-- 藤井正雄 『仏事の基礎知識』p.115 -->（『古事記伝』での表記は「波夫里（はふり）」と記す）。神道墓の形成は、後世の神道家によって大成されたものである。なお、[[縄文時代]]に[[貝塚]]に遺骸を捨てる風習があるが、これは一説<!-- 河野広道説 参考は後述書 p.132. -->に、貝塚自体が全ての生物の霊魂を他界へ送り返すための祭の場であり<!-- 河野説 参考後述書 -->、単なるゴミ捨て場ではなく、埋葬場として用いられたとするものがある<ref>[[渡辺誠 (考古学者)|渡辺誠]] 『縄文時代の知識 考古学シリーズ4』 [[東京美術]] ISBN 4-1087-0190-1 1983年 p.132.[[河野広道]]が[[アイヌ]]の習俗をヒントに得た説で、このため、親子兄弟の遺骸を棄てたとする。</ref>。
== 神道墓 == 基本的な構成は、仏式と同じであるが、神道では[[焼香]]を行わないので、[[香炉]]は要らない。また[[玉串]]を奉げる為の[[八足台]]が要る。 墓石の形は、細長い[[角柱]]型で、頂上部は[[四角錐]]になっている。この形は[[三種の神器]]の一つ[[天叢雲剣]]を表しているとされる。
墓石には「○○家奥都城」或いは「○○家奥津城」と刻む。墓石がない場合は墓標に「○○大人（刀自）命奥都城」と書く。神道では[[戒名]]はなく、姓名の下に、之霊・命・命霊・霊位などを付ける。
[[神社]]では通常[[墓地]]を所有していない。神式でお墓を建立する場合は公営や民営の[[霊園]]の[[墓地]]を買わなければならない。
日本軍人の墓は神道墓で建てられていることが多い。{{要出典|date=2014年8月}}
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
== 関連項目 == *[[御霊舎]] *[[生祀]] *[[神葬祭]]
{{Shinto-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:おくつき}} [[Category:墓]] [[Category:神道]]

revision.comment: ISBNが参照できなかったため、修正しました。
revision.text:
{{神道}} '''奥都城'''（おくつき）とは、[[上代]]の[[墓]]のこと。またそこから[[神道]]式の墓のこと。神道式の[[墓石]]に刻まれる文字でもある。'''奥津城'''、'''奥城'''とも書く。

== 神道墓 == 基本的な構成は、仏式と同じであるが、神道では[[焼香]]を行わないので、[[香炉]]は要らない。また[[玉串]]を奉げる為の[[八足台]]が要る。 墓石の形は、細長い[[角柱]]型で、頂上部は[[四角錐]]になっている。この形は[[三種の神器]]の一つ[[天叢雲剣]]を表しているとされる。
墓石には「○○家奥都城」或いは「○○家奥津城」と刻む。墓石がない場合は墓標に「○○大人（刀自）命奥都城」と書く。神道では[[戒名]]はなく、姓名の下に、之霊・命・命霊・霊位などを付ける。
[[神社]]では通常[[墓地]]を所有していない。神式でお墓を建立する場合は公営や民営の[[霊園]]の[[墓地]]を買わなければならない。
日本軍人の墓は神道墓で建てられていることが多い。{{要出典|date=2014年8月}}

{{神道}} '''奥都城'''（おくつき）とは、[[上代]]の[[墓]]のこと。またそこから[[神道]]式の墓のこと。神道式の[[墓石]]に刻まれる文字でもある。'''奥津城'''、'''奥城'''とも書く。
== 言葉の意味 == 「都・津（つ）」は、上代の[[格助詞]]「つ」に当てた[[万葉仮名]]で、「～の」の意味になる。「都」は、神官・氏子などを勤めた人の墓に使われる漢字で、「津」は一般信徒の墓に使われる。ただし、先祖に[[神官]]、[[氏子]]の[[役]]に従事した人がいる場合には「都」が使われることがある。またこれとは別に、地域により、どちらかの文字が広く用いられることもあり、一概ではない。

墓石には「○○家奥都城」或いは「○○家奥津城」と刻む。墓石がない場合は墓標に「○○大人（刀自）命奥都城」と書く。神道では[[戒名]]はなく、姓名の下に、之霊・命・命霊・霊位などを付ける。
[[神社]]では通常[[墓地]]を所有していない。神式でお墓を建立する場合は公営や民営の[[霊園]]の[[墓地]]を買わなければならない。
大日本帝国の軍人は先祖と同じ墓には入らず神道墓に祀られることが多かった。特に第二次大戦中の戦死者は日当たりの良い場所が割り当てられた<ref>鵜飼秀徳 (著) 『寺院消滅』 [[日経BP社]] ISBN 978-4822279172 p205</ref>。将校の墓石は姓名の前に所属（陸軍か海軍）と最終階級が掘られていることもある。

{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 05:07 (UTC)}} {{神道}} '''奥都城'''（おくつき）とは、[[上代]]の[[墓]]のこと。またそこから[[神道]]式の墓のこと。神道式の[[墓石]]に刻まれる文字でもある。'''奥津城'''、'''奥城'''とも書く。

== 言葉の意味 == 「都・津（つ）」は、上代の[[格助詞]]「つ」に当てた[[万葉仮名]]で、「～の」の意味になる。「都」は、神官・氏子などを勤めた人の墓に使われる漢字で、「津」は一般信徒の墓に使われる。ただし、先祖に[[神官]]、[[氏子]]の[[役]]に従事した人がいる場合には「都」が使われることがある。またこれとは別に、地域により、どちらかの文字が広く用いられることもあり、一概ではない。
「奥（おく）」とは、奥深い意の「奥」や「置く」を意味するといわれる。「城（き）」は、古代の「[[胆沢城]]」の「城」の用例にみるように棚・壁などで四辺を取り囲んだ一郭の場所をいい、また「[[柩]]（ひつぎ）」の意味もあるとされる。全体の意味としては、「奥深い所にあって外部から遮られた境域」ということであり、また「柩を置く場所」の意となる。
文献上の例として、『[[万葉集]]』に「奥都城」、『[[日本書紀]]』神代巻に「奥津棄戸（おくつ すたへ）」と記されている。本来の意は、死体遺棄による葬法を表しているものであり<ref>藤井正雄 『仏事の基礎知識』 講談社 初版1985年 ISBN 4-06-201068-2 p.116参考</ref>、一般民衆の死体が遺棄されていた事による（考古学上においても、古代日本において一般人は墓を築いた形跡はなく、遺棄された状態である<ref>『[[吾妻鏡]]』[[弘長]]元年（[[1261年]]）2月29日条には、幕府が関東諸侯において、「～死屍（しし）を路地に弃（す）つる事を禁制すべし」と定めている事からも、死体遺棄が中世前半の庶民にとっても一般的な行いであった事が分かる。</ref>）。『[[伊呂波字類抄]]』、『[[秦山集]]』、『[[伊勢物語]]』、『[[古事記伝]]』などの文献中に葬式の事を「はふる」と記しているが、これも遺棄を意味するものであり、奥都城と同様の意味であるとされる<!-- 藤井正雄 『仏事の基礎知識』p.115 -->（『古事記伝』での表記は「波夫里（はふり）」と記す）。神道墓の形成は、後世の神道家によって大成されたものである。なお、[[縄文時代]]に[[貝塚]]に遺骸を捨てる風習があるが、これは一説<!-- 河野広道説 参考は後述書 p.132. -->に、貝塚自体が全ての生物の霊魂を他界へ送り返すための祭の場であり<!-- 河野説 参考後述書 -->、単なるゴミ捨て場ではなく、埋葬場として用いられたとするものがある<ref>[[渡辺誠 (考古学者)|渡辺誠]] 『縄文時代の知識 考古学シリーズ4』 [[東京美術]] ISBN 4-8087-0190-1 1983年 p.132.[[河野広道]]が[[アイヌ]]の習俗をヒントに得た説で、このため、親子兄弟の遺骸を棄てたとする。</ref>。
== 神道墓 == 基本的な構成は、仏式と同じであるが、神道では[[焼香]]を行わないので、[[香炉]]は要らない。また[[玉串]]を奉げる為の[[八足台]]が要る。 墓石の形は、細長い[[角柱]]型で、頂上部は[[四角錐]]になっている。この形は[[三種の神器]]の一つ[[天叢雲剣]]を表しているとされる。
墓石には「○○家奥都城」或いは「○○家奥津城」と刻む。墓石がない場合は墓標に「○○大人（刀自）命奥都城」と書く。神道では[[戒名]]はなく、姓名の下に、之霊・命・命霊・霊位などを付ける。
[[神社]]では通常[[墓地]]を所有していない。神式でお墓を建立する場合は公営や民営の[[霊園]]の[[墓地]]を買わなければならない。
大日本帝国の軍人は先祖と同じ墓には入らず神道墓に祀られることが多かった。特に第二次大戦中の戦死者は日当たりの良い場所が割り当てられた<ref>鵜飼秀徳 (著) 『寺院消滅』 [[日経BP社]] ISBN 978-4822279172 p205</ref>。将校の墓石は姓名の前に所属（陸軍か海軍）と最終階級が掘られていることもある。
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
== 関連項目 == *[[御霊舎]] *[[生祀]] *[[神葬祭]] {{神道 横}} {{Shinto-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:おくつき}} [[Category:墓]] [[Category:神道]]

revision.text:
{{Cite sources clearly|date=August 28, 2017, 05:07 (UTC)}} '''Okutsuki''' (奥都城) refers to [[ancient|upper antiquity]] burial sites and, by extension, Shinto-style graves. It is also the term inscribed on gravestones in the Shinto style. Alternate spellings include '''Okutsushiro''' (奥津城) and '''Okujo''' (奥城).
== Meaning of the Term ==
"都" and "津 (tsu)" are [[Man'yōgana]] characters representing the ancient [[case particle]] "tsu," which means "of." The character "都" is used for graves of priests and ujiko (shrine parishioners), while "津" is for general believers. However, if ancestors served as [[priests]] or [[ujiko]] officials, "都" may also be used. Additionally, regional practices may favor one character over the other.
"奥 (oku)" conveys "deep" or "to place." "城 (ki)" refers to a space enclosed by walls or shelves, as in [[Isawa Castle]]'s usage, and also implies "coffin" ([[柩]]). Together, the term signifies "a secluded boundary shielded from the outside" or "a place to place a coffin."
In historical texts, the term "Okutsuki" appears in the ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}'' and "Okutsusutae" (奥津棄戸) in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}''. Initially, it described funerary practices involving the abandonment of corpses<ref>Fujii Masao, ''Basic Knowledge of Buddhist Rites'', Kodansha, 1985, ISBN 4-06-201068-2, p.116.</ref>. Commoners' remains were often left exposed, as evidenced archaeologically in ancient Japan, where there are no burial structures for ordinary people<ref>''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}}'', entry for February 29, 1261 (Kōchō 1): The shogunate forbade corpse abandonment, a practice common even among medieval commoners.</ref>. Terms such as "hafuru" in texts like ''{{ill|Iroha Jirui Shō|ja|伊呂波字類抄}}'', ''{{ill|Shinzan-shū|ja|秦山集}}'', and ''{{ill|The Tales of Ise|ja|伊勢物語}}'' similarly denote abandonment and share meanings with "Okutsuki"<!-- Fujii Masao, p.115 -->. Over time, Shinto-style graves evolved under the influence of later Shinto scholars. During the [[Jōmon period]], disposing of remains in shell mounds served as a ritual to send spirits to the afterlife<ref>Makoto Watanabe, ''Jōmon Period Knowledge: Archaeology Series 4'', Tokyo Bijutsu, ISBN 4-8087-0190-1, 1983, p.132.</ref>.
== Shinto Graves ==
Shinto graves follow structures similar to Buddhist ones but omit incense burning, requiring no [[incense burners]]. Instead, an [[eight-legged stand]] (八足台) is used to offer [[sakaki]] branches.
Gravestones are tall, slender rectangular pillars with pyramid-shaped tops, symbolizing the [[Ame no Murakumo no Tsurugi]], one of the [[Three Sacred Treasures]]. Gravestones are inscribed with "○○ Family Okutsuki" or "○○ Family Okutsushiro." If no gravestone exists, the grave marker is inscribed with "○○ Elder (刀自) Spirit Okutsuki." In Shinto, there are no [[posthumous names]]; instead, the deceased's name is followed by "no Mitama," "no Mikoto," "Reisō," or "Reii."
[[Shrines]] typically do not own cemeteries. To establish a Shinto grave, families must purchase plots in public or private cemeteries.
Military personnel of the [[Empire of Japan]] were often enshrined in Shinto graves separate from their family's ancestral plots. During [[World War II]], sunny locations were allocated for the war dead<ref>Hideyoshi Ukai, ''Temple Disappearance,'' Nikkei BP, ISBN 978-4822279172, p.205.</ref>. Gravestones for officers sometimes included their branch (Army or Navy) and final rank before their names.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Related Articles == * [[Mitamaya]] * [[Seishi (Shinto)]] * [[Shinsōsai]]
{{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}}
[[Category:Burial sites]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[ja:奥都城]]

During [[World War II]], sunny locations were allocated for the war dead<ref>Hideyoshi Ukai, ''Temple Disappearance,'' Nikkei BP, ISBN 978-4822279172, p.205.</ref>. Gravestones for officers sometimes included their branch (Army or Navy) and final rank before their names.

revision.text:
{{Cite sources clearly|date=August 28, 2017, 05:07 (UTC)}} '''Okutsuki''' (奥都城) refers to [[ancient|upper antiquity]] burial sites and, by extension, Shinto-style graves. It is also the term inscribed on gravestones in the Shinto style. Alternate spellings include '''Okutsushiro''' (奥津城) and '''Okujo''' (奥城).
== Meaning of the Term ==
"都" and "津 (tsu)" are [[Man'yōgana]] characters representing the ancient [[case particle]] "tsu," which means "of." The character "都" is used for graves of priests and ujiko (shrine parishioners), while "津" is for general believers. However, if ancestors served as [[priests]] or [[ujiko]] officials, "都" may also be used. Additionally, regional practices may favor one character over the other.
"奥 (oku)" conveys "deep" or "to place." "城 (ki)" refers to a space enclosed by walls or shelves, as in [[Isawa Castle]]'s usage, and also implies "coffin" ([[柩]]). Together, the term signifies "a secluded boundary shielded from the outside" or "a place to place a coffin."
In historical texts, the term "Okutsuki" appears in the ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}'' and "Okutsusutae" (奥津棄戸) in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}''. Initially, it described funerary practices involving the abandonment of corpses<ref>Fujii Masao, ''Basic Knowledge of Buddhist Rites'', Kodansha, 1985, ISBN 4-06-201068-2, p.116.</ref>. Commoners' remains were often left exposed, as evidenced archaeologically in ancient Japan, where there are no burial structures for ordinary people<ref>''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}}'', entry for February 29, 1261 (Kōchō 1): The shogunate forbade corpse abandonment, a practice common even among medieval commoners.</ref>. Terms such as "hafuru" in texts like ''{{ill|Iroha Jirui Shō|ja|伊呂波字類抄}}'', ''{{ill|Shinzan-shū|ja|秦山集}}'', and ''{{ill|The Tales of Ise|ja|伊勢物語}}'' similarly denote abandonment and share meanings with "Okutsuki"<!-- Fujii Masao, p.115 -->. Over time, Shinto-style graves evolved under the influence of later Shinto scholars. During the [[Jōmon period]], disposing of remains in shell mounds served as a ritual to send spirits to the afterlife<ref>Makoto Watanabe, ''Jōmon Period Knowledge: Archaeology Series 4'', Tokyo Bijutsu, ISBN 4-8087-0190-1, 1983, p.132.</ref>.
== Shinto Graves ==
Shinto graves follow structures similar to Buddhist ones but omit incense burning, requiring no [[incense burners]]. Instead, an [[eight-legged stand]] (八足台) is used to offer [[sakaki]] branches.
Gravestones are tall, slender rectangular pillars with pyramid-shaped tops, symbolizing the [[Ame no Murakumo no Tsurugi]], one of the [[Three Sacred Treasures]]. Gravestones are inscribed with "○○ Family Okutsuki" or "○○ Family Okutsushiro." If no gravestone exists, the grave marker is inscribed with "○○ Elder (刀自) Spirit Okutsuki." In Shinto, there are no [[posthumous names]]; instead, the deceased's name is followed by "no Mitama," "no Mikoto," "Reisō," or "Reii."
[[Shrines]] typically do not own cemeteries. To establish a Shinto grave, families must purchase plots in public or private cemeteries.
Military personnel of the [[Empire of Japan]] were often enshrined in Shinto graves separate from their family's ancestral plots. During [[World War II]], sunny locations were allocated for the war dead<ref>Hideyoshi Ukai, ''Temple Disappearance,'' Nikkei BP, ISBN 978-4822279172, p.205.</ref>. Gravestones for officers sometimes included their branch (Army or Navy) and final rank before their names.

Terms such as "hafuru" in texts like ''{{ill|Iroha Jirui Shō|ja|伊呂波字類抄}}'', ''{{ill|Shinzan-shū|ja|秦山集}}'', and ''{{ill|The Tales of Ise|ja|伊勢物語}}'' similarly denote abandonment and share meanings with "Okutsuki"<!-- Fujii Masao, p.115 -->. Over time, Shinto-style graves evolved under the influence of later Shinto scholars. During the [[Jōmon period]], disposing of remains in shell mounds served as a ritual to send spirits to the afterlife<ref>Makoto Watanabe, ''Jōmon Period Knowledge: Archaeology Series 4'', Tokyo Bijutsu, ISBN 4-8087-0190-1, 1983, p.132.</ref>.
== Shinto Graves ==
Shinto graves follow structures similar to Buddhist ones but omit incense burning, requiring no [[incense burners]]. Instead, an [[eight-legged stand]] (八足台) is used to offer [[sakaki]] branches.
Gravestones are tall, slender rectangular pillars with pyramid-shaped tops, symbolizing the [[Ame no Murakumo no Tsurugi]], one of the [[Three Sacred Treasures]]. Gravestones are inscribed with "○○ Family Okutsuki" or "○○ Family Okutsushiro." If no gravestone exists, the grave marker is inscribed with "○○ Elder (刀自) Spirit Okutsuki." In Shinto, there are no [[posthumous names]]; instead, the deceased's name is followed by "no Mitama," "no Mikoto," "Reisō," or "Reii."
[[Shrines]] typically do not own cemeteries. To establish a Shinto grave, families must purchase plots in public or private cemeteries.
Military personnel of the [[Empire of Japan]] were often enshrined in Shinto graves separate from their family's ancestral plots. During [[World War II]], sunny locations were allocated for the war dead<ref>Hideyoshi Ukai, ''Temple Disappearance,'' Nikkei BP, ISBN 978-4822279172, p.205.</ref>. Gravestones for officers sometimes included their branch (Army or Navy) and final rank before their names.

revision.text:
{{Cite sources clearly|date=August 28, 2017, 05:07 (UTC)}} '''Okutsuki''' (奥都城) refers to [[ancient|upper antiquity]] burial sites and, by extension, Shinto-style graves. It is also the term inscribed on gravestones in the Shinto style. Alternate spellings include '''Okutsushiro''' (奥津城) and '''Okujo''' (奥城).
== Meaning of the Term ==
"都" and "津 (tsu)" are [[Man'yōgana]] characters representing the ancient [[case particle]] "tsu," which means "of." The character "都" is used for graves of priests and ujiko (shrine parishioners), while "津" is for general believers. However, if ancestors served as [[priests]] or [[ujiko]] officials, "都" may also be used. Additionally, regional practices may favor one character over the other.
"奥 (oku)" conveys "deep" or "to place." "城 (ki)" refers to a space enclosed by walls or shelves, as in [[Isawa Castle]]'s usage, and also implies "coffin" ([[柩]]). Together, the term signifies "a secluded boundary shielded from the outside" or "a place to place a coffin."
In historical texts, the term "Okutsuki" appears in the ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}'' and "Okutsusutae" (奥津棄戸) in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}''. Initially, it described funerary practices involving the abandonment of corpses<ref>Fujii Masao, ''Basic Knowledge of Buddhist Rites'', Kodansha, 1985, ISBN 4-06-201068-2, p.116.</ref>. Commoners' remains were often left exposed, as evidenced archaeologically in ancient Japan, where there are no burial structures for ordinary people<ref>''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}}'', entry for February 29, 1261 (Kōchō 1): The shogunate forbade corpse abandonment, a practice common even among medieval commoners.</ref>. Terms such as "hafuru" in texts like ''{{ill|Iroha Jirui Shō|ja|伊呂波字類抄}}'', ''{{ill|Shinzan-shū|ja|秦山集}}'', and ''{{ill|The Tales of Ise|ja|伊勢物語}}'' similarly denote abandonment and share meanings with "Okutsuki"<!-- Fujii Masao, p.115 -->. Over time, Shinto-style graves evolved under the influence of later Shinto scholars. During the [[Jōmon period]], disposing of remains in shell mounds served as a ritual to send spirits to the afterlife<ref>Makoto Watanabe, ''Jōmon Period Knowledge: Archaeology Series 4'', Tokyo Bijutsu, ISBN 4-8087-0190-1, 1983, p.132.</ref>.
== Shinto Graves ==
Shinto graves follow structures similar to Buddhist ones but omit incense burning, requiring no [[incense burners]]. Instead, an [[eight-legged stand]] (八足台) is used to offer [[sakaki]] branches.
Gravestones are tall, slender rectangular pillars with pyramid-shaped tops, symbolizing the [[Ame no Murakumo no Tsurugi]], one of the [[Three Sacred Treasures]]. Gravestones are inscribed with "○○ Family Okutsuki" or "○○ Family Okutsushiro." If no gravestone exists, the grave marker is inscribed with "○○ Elder (刀自) Spirit Okutsuki." In Shinto, there are no [[posthumous names]]; instead, the deceased's name is followed by "no Mitama," "no Mikoto," "Reisō," or "Reii."
[[Shrines]] typically do not own cemeteries. To establish a Shinto grave, families must purchase plots in public or private cemeteries.
Military personnel of the [[Empire of Japan]] were often enshrined in Shinto graves separate from their family's ancestral plots. During [[World War II]], sunny locations were allocated for the war dead<ref>Hideyoshi Ukai, ''Temple Disappearance,'' Nikkei BP, ISBN 978-4822279172, p.205.</ref>. Gravestones for officers sometimes included their branch (Army or Navy) and final rank before their names.

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Completion tier 1]] to [[Category:qq-{{subst:PAGENAME}}-qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Cite sources clearly|date=August 28, 2017, 05:07 (UTC)}} '''Okutsuki''' (奥都城) refers to [[ancient|upper antiquity]] burial sites and, by extension, Shinto-style graves. It is also the term inscribed on gravestones in the Shinto style. Alternate spellings include '''Okutsushiro''' (奥津城) and '''Okujo''' (奥城).
== Meaning of the Term ==
"都" and "津 (tsu)" are [[Man'yōgana]] characters representing the ancient [[case particle]] "tsu," which means "of." The character "都" is used for graves of priests and ujiko (shrine parishioners), while "津" is for general believers. However, if ancestors served as [[priests]] or [[ujiko]] officials, "都" may also be used. Additionally, regional practices may favor one character over the other.
"奥 (oku)" conveys "deep" or "to place." "城 (ki)" refers to a space enclosed by walls or shelves, as in [[Isawa Castle]]'s usage, and also implies "coffin" ([[柩]]). Together, the term signifies "a secluded boundary shielded from the outside" or "a place to place a coffin."
In historical texts, the term "Okutsuki" appears in the ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}'' and "Okutsusutae" (奥津棄戸) in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}''. Initially, it described funerary practices involving the abandonment of corpses<ref>Fujii Masao, ''Basic Knowledge of Buddhist Rites'', Kodansha, 1985, ISBN 4-06-201068-2, p.116.</ref>. Commoners' remains were often left exposed, as evidenced archaeologically in ancient Japan, where there are no burial structures for ordinary people<ref>''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}}'', entry for February 29, 1261 (Kōchō 1): The shogunate forbade corpse abandonment, a practice common even among medieval commoners.</ref>. Terms such as "hafuru" in texts like ''{{ill|Iroha Jirui Shō|ja|伊呂波字類抄}}'', ''{{ill|Shinzan-shū|ja|秦山集}}'', and ''{{ill|The Tales of Ise|ja|伊勢物語}}'' similarly denote abandonment and share meanings with "Okutsuki"<!-- Fujii Masao, p.115 -->. Over time, Shinto-style graves evolved under the influence of later Shinto scholars. During the [[Jōmon period]], disposing of remains in shell mounds served as a ritual to send spirits to the afterlife<ref>Makoto Watanabe, ''Jōmon Period Knowledge: Archaeology Series 4'', Tokyo Bijutsu, ISBN 4-8087-0190-1, 1983, p.132.</ref>.
== Shinto Graves ==
Shinto graves follow structures similar to Buddhist ones but omit incense burning, requiring no [[incense burners]]. Instead, an [[eight-legged stand]] (八足台) is used to offer [[sakaki]] branches.
Gravestones are tall, slender rectangular pillars with pyramid-shaped tops, symbolizing the [[Ame no Murakumo no Tsurugi]], one of the [[Three Sacred Treasures]]. Gravestones are inscribed with "○○ Family Okutsuki" or "○○ Family Okutsushiro." If no gravestone exists, the grave marker is inscribed with "○○ Elder (刀自) Spirit Okutsuki." In Shinto, there are no [[posthumous names]]; instead, the deceased's name is followed by "no Mitama," "no Mikoto," "Reisō," or "Reii."
[[Shrines]] typically do not own cemeteries. To establish a Shinto grave, families must purchase plots in public or private cemeteries.
Military personnel of the [[Empire of Japan]] were often enshrined in Shinto graves separate from their family's ancestral plots. During [[World War II]], sunny locations were allocated for the war dead<ref>Hideyoshi Ukai, ''Temple Disappearance,'' Nikkei BP, ISBN 978-4822279172, p.205.</ref>. Gravestones for officers sometimes included their branch (Army or Navy) and final rank before their names.

In historical texts, the term "Okutsuki" appears in the ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}'' and "Okutsusutae" (奥津棄戸) in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}''. Initially, it described funerary practices involving the abandonment of corpses<ref>Fujii Masao, ''Basic Knowledge of Buddhist Rites'', Kodansha, 1985, ISBN 4-06-201068-2, p.116.</ref>. Commoners' remains were often left exposed, as evidenced archaeologically in ancient Japan, where there are no burial structures for ordinary people<ref>''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}}'', entry for February 29, 1261 (Kōchō 1): The shogunate forbade corpse abandonment, a practice common even among medieval commoners.</ref>. Terms such as "hafuru" in texts like ''{{ill|Iroha Jirui Shō|ja|伊呂波字類抄}}'', ''{{ill|Shinzan-shū|ja|秦山集}}'', and ''{{ill|The Tales of Ise|ja|伊勢物語}}'' similarly denote abandonment and share meanings with "Okutsuki"<!-- Fujii Masao, p.115 -->. Over time, Shinto-style graves evolved under the influence of later Shinto scholars. During the [[Jōmon period]], disposing of remains in shell mounds served as a ritual to send spirits to the afterlife<ref>Makoto Watanabe, ''Jōmon Period Knowledge: Archaeology Series 4'', Tokyo Bijutsu, ISBN 4-8087-0190-1, 1983, p.132.</ref>.
== Shinto Graves ==
Shinto graves follow structures similar to Buddhist ones but omit incense burning, requiring no [[incense burners]]. Instead, an [[eight-legged stand]] (八足台) is used to offer [[sakaki]] branches.
Gravestones are tall, slender rectangular pillars with pyramid-shaped tops, symbolizing the [[Ame no Murakumo no Tsurugi]], one of the [[Three Sacred Treasures]]. Gravestones are inscribed with "○○ Family Okutsuki" or "○○ Family Okutsushiro." If no gravestone exists, the grave marker is inscribed with "○○ Elder (刀自) Spirit Okutsuki." In Shinto, there are no [[posthumous names]]; instead, the deceased's name is followed by "no Mitama," "no Mikoto," "Reisō," or "Reii."
[[Shrines]] typically do not own cemeteries. To establish a Shinto grave, families must purchase plots in public or private cemeteries.
Military personnel of the [[Empire of Japan]] were often enshrined in Shinto graves separate from their family's ancestral plots. During [[World War II]], sunny locations were allocated for the war dead<ref>Hideyoshi Ukai, ''Temple Disappearance,'' Nikkei BP, ISBN 978-4822279172, p.205.</ref>. Gravestones for officers sometimes included their branch (Army or Navy) and final rank before their names.

{{Cite sources clearly|date=August 28, 2017, 05:07 (UTC)}} '''Okutsuki''' (奥都城) refers to [[ancient|upper antiquity]] burial sites and, by extension, Shinto-style graves. It is also the term inscribed on gravestones in the Shinto style. Alternate spellings include '''Okutsushiro''' (奥津城) and '''Okujo''' (奥城).
== Meaning of the Term ==
"都" and "津 (tsu)" are [[Man'yōgana]] characters representing the ancient [[case particle]] "tsu," which means "of." The character "都" is used for graves of priests and ujiko (shrine parishioners), while "津" is for general believers. However, if ancestors served as [[priests]] or [[ujiko]] officials, "都" may also be used. Additionally, regional practices may favor one character over the other.
"奥 (oku)" conveys "deep" or "to place." "城 (ki)" refers to a space enclosed by walls or shelves, as in [[Isawa Castle]]'s usage, and also implies "coffin" ([[柩]]). Together, the term signifies "a secluded boundary shielded from the outside" or "a place to place a coffin."
In historical texts, the term "Okutsuki" appears in the ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}'' and "Okutsusutae" (奥津棄戸) in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}''. Initially, it described funerary practices involving the abandonment of corpses<ref>Fujii Masao, ''Basic Knowledge of Buddhist Rites'', Kodansha, 1985, ISBN 4-06-201068-2, p.116.</ref>. Commoners' remains were often left exposed, as evidenced archaeologically in ancient Japan, where there are no burial structures for ordinary people<ref>''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}}'', entry for February 29, 1261 (Kōchō 1): The shogunate forbade corpse abandonment, a practice common even among medieval commoners.</ref>. Terms such as "hafuru" in texts like ''{{ill|Iroha Jirui Shō|ja|伊呂波字類抄}}'', ''{{ill|Shinzan-shū|ja|秦山集}}'', and ''{{ill|The Tales of Ise|ja|伊勢物語}}'' similarly denote abandonment and share meanings with "Okutsuki"<!-- Fujii Masao, p.115 -->. Over time, Shinto-style graves evolved under the influence of later Shinto scholars. During the [[Jōmon period]], disposing of remains in shell mounds served as a ritual to send spirits to the afterlife<ref>Makoto Watanabe, ''Jōmon Period Knowledge: Archaeology Series 4'', Tokyo Bijutsu, ISBN 4-8087-0190-1, 1983, p.132.</ref>.
== Shinto Graves ==
Shinto graves follow structures similar to Buddhist ones but omit incense burning, requiring no [[incense burners]]. Instead, an [[eight-legged stand]] (八足台) is used to offer [[sakaki]] branches.
Gravestones are tall, slender rectangular pillars with pyramid-shaped tops, symbolizing the [[Ame no Murakumo no Tsurugi]], one of the [[Three Sacred Treasures]]. Gravestones are inscribed with "○○ Family Okutsuki" or "○○ Family Okutsushiro." If no gravestone exists, the grave marker is inscribed with "○○ Elder (刀自) Spirit Okutsuki." In Shinto, there are no [[posthumous names]]; instead, the deceased's name is followed by "no Mitama," "no Mikoto," "Reisō," or "Reii."
[[Shrines]] typically do not own cemeteries. To establish a Shinto grave, families must purchase plots in public or private cemeteries.
Military personnel of the [[Empire of Japan]] were often enshrined in Shinto graves separate from their family's ancestral plots. During [[World War II]], sunny locations were allocated for the war dead<ref>Hideyoshi Ukai, ''Temple Disappearance,'' Nikkei BP, ISBN 978-4822279172, p.205.</ref>. Gravestones for officers sometimes included their branch (Army or Navy) and final rank before their names.

Commoners' remains were often left exposed, as evidenced archaeologically in ancient Japan, where there are no burial structures for ordinary people<ref>''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}}'', entry for February 29, 1261 (Kōchō 1): The shogunate forbade corpse abandonment, a practice common even among medieval commoners.</ref>. Terms such as "hafuru" in texts like ''{{ill|Iroha Jirui Shō|ja|伊呂波字類抄}}'', ''{{ill|Shinzan-shū|ja|秦山集}}'', and ''{{ill|The Tales of Ise|ja|伊勢物語}}'' similarly denote abandonment and share meanings with "Okutsuki"<!-- Fujii Masao, p.115 -->. Over time, Shinto-style graves evolved under the influence of later Shinto scholars. During the [[Jōmon period]], disposing of remains in shell mounds served as a ritual to send spirits to the afterlife<ref>Makoto Watanabe, ''Jōmon Period Knowledge: Archaeology Series 4'', Tokyo Bijutsu, ISBN 4-8087-0190-1, 1983, p.132.</ref>.
== Shinto Graves ==
Shinto graves follow structures similar to Buddhist ones but omit incense burning, requiring no [[incense burners]]. Instead, an [[eight-legged stand]] (八足台) is used to offer [[sakaki]] branches.
Gravestones are tall, slender rectangular pillars with pyramid-shaped tops, symbolizing the [[Ame no Murakumo no Tsurugi]], one of the [[Three Sacred Treasures]]. Gravestones are inscribed with "○○ Family Okutsuki" or "○○ Family Okutsushiro." If no gravestone exists, the grave marker is inscribed with "○○ Elder (刀自) Spirit Okutsuki." In Shinto, there are no [[posthumous names]]; instead, the deceased's name is followed by "no Mitama," "no Mikoto," "Reisō," or "Reii."
[[Shrines]] typically do not own cemeteries. To establish a Shinto grave, families must purchase plots in public or private cemeteries.
Military personnel of the [[Empire of Japan]] were often enshrined in Shinto graves separate from their family's ancestral plots. During [[World War II]], sunny locations were allocated for the war dead<ref>Hideyoshi Ukai, ''Temple Disappearance,'' Nikkei BP, ISBN 978-4822279172, p.205.</ref>. Gravestones for officers sometimes included their branch (Army or Navy) and final rank before their names.

The character "都" is used for graves of priests and ujiko (shrine parishioners), while "津" is for general believers. revision.text:
{{Cite sources clearly|date=August 28, 2017, 05:07 (UTC)}} '''Okutsuki''' (奥都城) refers to [[ancient|upper antiquity]] burial sites and, by extension, Shinto-style graves. It is also the term inscribed on gravestones in the Shinto style. Alternate spellings include '''Okutsushiro''' (奥津城) and '''Okujo''' (奥城).
== Meaning of the Term ==
"都" and "津 (tsu)" are [[Man'yōgana]] characters representing the ancient [[case particle]] "tsu," which means "of." The character "都" is used for graves of priests and ujiko (shrine parishioners), while "津" is for general believers. However, if ancestors served as [[priests]] or [[ujiko]] officials, "都" may also be used. Additionally, regional practices may favor one character over the other.
"奥 (oku)" conveys "deep" or "to place." "城 (ki)" refers to a space enclosed by walls or shelves, as in [[Isawa Castle]]'s usage, and also implies "coffin" ([[柩]]). Together, the term signifies "a secluded boundary shielded from the outside" or "a place to place a coffin."
In historical texts, the term "Okutsuki" appears in the ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}'' and "Okutsusutae" (奥津棄戸) in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}''. Initially, it described funerary practices involving the abandonment of corpses<ref>Fujii Masao, ''Basic Knowledge of Buddhist Rites'', Kodansha, 1985, ISBN 4-06-201068-2, p.116.</ref>. Commoners' remains were often left exposed, as evidenced archaeologically in ancient Japan, where there are no burial structures for ordinary people<ref>''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}}'', entry for February 29, 1261 (Kōchō 1): The shogunate forbade corpse abandonment, a practice common even among medieval commoners.</ref>. Terms such as "hafuru" in texts like ''{{ill|Iroha Jirui Shō|ja|伊呂波字類抄}}'', ''{{ill|Shinzan-shū|ja|秦山集}}'', and ''{{ill|The Tales of Ise|ja|伊勢物語}}'' similarly denote abandonment and share meanings with "Okutsuki"<!-- Fujii Masao, p.115 -->. Over time, Shinto-style graves evolved under the influence of later Shinto scholars. During the [[Jōmon period]], disposing of remains in shell mounds served as a ritual to send spirits to the afterlife<ref>Makoto Watanabe, ''Jōmon Period Knowledge: Archaeology Series 4'', Tokyo Bijutsu, ISBN 4-8087-0190-1, 1983, p.132.</ref>.
== Shinto Graves ==
Shinto graves follow structures similar to Buddhist ones but omit incense burning, requiring no [[incense burners]]. Instead, an [[eight-legged stand]] (八足台) is used to offer [[sakaki]] branches.
Gravestones are tall, slender rectangular pillars with pyramid-shaped tops, symbolizing the [[Ame no Murakumo no Tsurugi]], one of the [[Three Sacred Treasures]]. Gravestones are inscribed with "○○ Family Okutsuki" or "○○ Family Okutsushiro." If no gravestone exists, the grave marker is inscribed with "○○ Elder (刀自) Spirit Okutsuki." In Shinto, there are no [[posthumous names]]; instead, the deceased's name is followed by "no Mitama," "no Mikoto," "Reisō," or "Reii."
[[Shrines]] typically do not own cemeteries. To establish a Shinto grave, families must purchase plots in public or private cemeteries.
Military personnel of the [[Empire of Japan]] were often enshrined in Shinto graves separate from their family's ancestral plots. During [[World War II]], sunny locations were allocated for the war dead<ref>Hideyoshi Ukai, ''Temple Disappearance,'' Nikkei BP, ISBN 978-4822279172, p.205.</ref>. Gravestones for officers sometimes included their branch (Army or Navy) and final rank before their names.

Military personnel of the [[Empire of Japan]] were often enshrined in Shinto graves separate from their family's ancestral plots. During [[World War II]], sunny locations were allocated for the war dead<ref>Hideyoshi Ukai, ''Temple Disappearance,'' Nikkei BP, ISBN 978-4822279172, p.205.</ref>. Gravestones for officers sometimes included their branch (Army or Navy) and final rank before their names.

"奥 (oku)" conveys "deep" or "to place." "城 (ki)" refers to a space enclosed by walls or shelves, as in [[Isawa Castle]]'s usage, and also implies "coffin" ([[柩]]). Together, the term signifies "a secluded boundary shielded from the outside" or "a place to place a coffin."

Initially, it described funerary practices involving the abandonment of corpses<ref>Fujii Masao, ''Basic Knowledge of Buddhist Rites'', Kodansha, 1985, ISBN 4-06-201068-2, p.qq6.</ref>. Commoners' remains were often left exposed, as evidenced archaeologically in ancient Japan, where there are no burial structures for ordinary people<ref>''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}}'', entry for February 29, 1261 (Kōchō 1): The shogunate forbade corpse abandonment, a practice common even among medieval commoners.</ref>. Terms such as "hafuru" in texts like ''{{ill|Iroha Jirui Shō|ja|伊呂波字類抄}}'', ''{{ill|Shinzan-shū|ja|秦山集}}'', and ''{{ill|The Tales of Ise|ja|伊勢物語}}'' similarly denote abandonment and share meanings with "Okutsuki"<!-- Fujii Masao, p.qq5 -->. Over time, Shinto-style graves evolved under the influence of later Shinto scholars. During the [[Jōmon period]], disposing of remains in shell mounds served as a ritual to send spirits to the afterlife<ref>Makoto Watanabe, ''Jōmon Period Knowledge: Archaeology Series 4'', Tokyo Bijutsu, ISBN 4-8087-0190-1, 1983, p.132.</ref>.
== Shinto Graves ==
Shinto graves follow structures similar to Buddhist ones but omit incense burning, requiring no [[incense burners]]. Instead, an [[eight-legged stand]] (八足台) is used to offer [[sakaki]] branches.
Gravestones are tall, slender rectangular pillars with pyramid-shaped tops, symbolizing the [[Ame no Murakumo no Tsurugi]], one of the [[Three Sacred Treasures]]. Gravestones are inscribed with "○○ Family Okutsuki" or "○○ Family Okutsushiro." If no gravestone exists, the grave marker is inscribed with "○○ Elder (刀自) Spirit Okutsuki." In Shinto, there are no [[posthumous names]]; instead, the deceased's name is followed by "no Mitama," "no Mikoto," "Reisō," or "Reii."
[[Shrines]] typically do not own cemeteries. To establish a Shinto grave, families must purchase plots in public or private cemeteries.
Military personnel of the [[Empire of Japan]] were often enshrined in Shinto graves separate from their family's ancestral plots. During [[World War II]], sunny locations were allocated for the war dead<ref>Hideyoshi Ukai, ''Temple Disappearance,'' Nikkei BP, ISBN 978-4822279172, p.205.</ref>. Gravestones for officers sometimes included their branch (Army or Navy) and final rank before their names.

{{Cite sources clearly|date=August 28, 2017, 05:07 (UTC)}} '''Okutsuki''' (奥都城) refers to [[ancient|upper antiquity]] burial sites and, by extension, Shinto-style graves. It is also the term inscribed on gravestones in the Shinto style. Alternate spellings include '''Okutsushiro''' (奥津城) and '''Okujo''' (奥城).
== Meaning of the Term ==
"都" and "津 (tsu)" are [[Man'yōgana]] characters representing the ancient [[case particle]] "tsu," which means "of." The character "都" is used for graves of priests and ujiko (shrine parishioners), while "津" is for general believers. However, if ancestors served as [[priests]] or [[ujiko]] officials, "都" may also be used. Additionally, regional practices may favor one character over the other.
"奥 (oku)" conveys "deep" or "to place." "城 (ki)" refers to a space enclosed by walls or shelves, as in [[Isawa Castle]]'s usage, and also implies "coffin" ([[柩]]). Together, the term signifies "a secluded boundary shielded from the outside" or "a place to place a coffin."
In historical texts, the term "Okutsuki" appears in the ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}'' and "Okutsusutae" (奥津棄戸) in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}''. Initially, it described funerary practices involving the abandonment of corpses<ref>Fujii Masao, ''Basic Knowledge of Buddhist Rites'', Kodansha, 1985, ISBN 4-06-201068-2, p.qq6.</ref>. Commoners' remains were often left exposed, as evidenced archaeologically in ancient Japan, where there are no burial structures for ordinary people<ref>''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}}'', entry for February 29, 1261 (Kōchō 1): The shogunate forbade corpse abandonment, a practice common even among medieval commoners.</ref>. Terms such as "hafuru" in texts like ''{{ill|Iroha Jirui Shō|ja|伊呂波字類抄}}'', ''{{ill|Shinzan-shū|ja|秦山集}}'', and ''{{ill|The Tales of Ise|ja|伊勢物語}}'' similarly denote abandonment and share meanings with "Okutsuki"<!-- Fujii Masao, p.qq5 -->. Over time, Shinto-style graves evolved under the influence of later Shinto scholars. During the [[Jōmon period]], disposing of remains in shell mounds served as a ritual to send spirits to the afterlife<ref>Makoto Watanabe, ''Jōmon Period Knowledge: Archaeology Series 4'', Tokyo Bijutsu, ISBN 4-8087-0190-1, 1983, p.132.</ref>.

Gravestones are tall, slender rectangular pillars with pyramid-shaped tops, symbolizing the [[Ame no Murakumo no Tsurugi]], one of the [[Three Sacred Treasures]]. Gravestones are inscribed with "○○ Family Okutsuki" or "○○ Family Okutsushiro." If no gravestone exists, the grave marker is inscribed with "○○ Elder (刀自) Spirit Okutsuki." In Shinto, there are no [[posthumous names]]; instead, the deceased's name is followed by "no Mitama," "no Mikoto," "Reisō," or "Reii."
[[Shrines]] typically do not own cemeteries. To establish a Shinto grave, families must purchase plots in public or private cemeteries.
Military personnel of the [[Empire of Japan]] were often enshrined in Shinto graves separate from their family's ancestral plots. During [[World War II]], sunny locations were allocated for the war dead<ref>Hideyoshi Ukai, ''Temple Disappearance,'' Nikkei BP, ISBN 978-4822279172, p.205.</ref>. Gravestones for officers sometimes included their branch (Army or Navy) and final rank before their names.

In Shinto, there are no [[posthumous names]]; instead, the deceased's name is followed by "no Mitama," "no Mikoto," "Reisō," or "Reii."
[[Shrines]] typically do not own cemeteries. To establish a Shinto grave, families must purchase plots in public or private cemeteries.
Military personnel of the [[Empire of Japan]] were often enshrined in Shinto graves separate from their family's ancestral plots. During [[World War II]], sunny locations were allocated for the war dead<ref>Hideyoshi Ukai, ''Temple Disappearance,'' Nikkei BP, ISBN 978-4822279172, p.205.</ref>. Gravestones for officers sometimes included their branch (Army or Navy) and final rank before their names.

{{Cite sources clearly|date=August 28, 2017, 05:07 (UTC)}} '''Okutsuki''' (奥都城) refers to [[ancient|upper antiquity]] burial sites and, by extension, Shinto-style graves. It is also the term inscribed on gravestones in the Shinto style. Alternate spellings include '''Okutsushiro''' (奥津城) and '''Okujo''' (奥城).
== Meaning of the Term ==
"都" and "津 (tsu)" are [[Man'yōgana]] characters representing the ancient [[case particle]] "tsu," which means "of." The character "都" is used for graves of priests and ujiko (shrine parishioners), while "津" is for general believers. However, if ancestors served as [[priests]] or [[ujiko]] officials, "都" may also be used. Additionally, regional practices may favor one character over the other.
"奥 (oku)" conveys "deep" or "to place." "城 (ki)" refers to a space enclosed by walls or shelves, as in [[Isawa Castle]]'s usage, and also implies "coffin" ([[柩]]). Together, the term signifies "a secluded boundary shielded from the outside" or "a place to place a coffin."
In historical texts, the term "Okutsuki" appears in the ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}'' and "Okutsusutae" (奥津棄戸) in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}''. Initially, it described funerary practices involving the abandonment of corpses<ref>Fujii Masao, ''Basic Knowledge of Buddhist Rites'', Kodansha, 1985, ISBN 4-06-201068-2, p.qq6.</ref>. Commoners' remains were often left exposed, as evidenced archaeologically in ancient Japan, where there are no burial structures for ordinary people<ref>''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}}'', entry for February 29, 1261 (Kōchō 1): The shogunate forbade corpse abandonment, a practice common even among medieval commoners.</ref>. Terms such as "hafuru" in texts like ''{{ill|Iroha Jirui Shō|ja|伊呂波字類抄}}'', ''{{ill|Shinzan-shū|ja|秦山集}}'', and ''{{ill|The Tales of Ise|ja|伊勢物語}}'' similarly denote abandonment and share meanings with "Okutsuki"<!-- Fujii Masao, p.qq5 -->. Over time, Shinto-style graves evolved under the influence of later Shinto scholars. During the [[Jōmon period]], disposing of remains in shell mounds served as a ritual to send spirits to the afterlife<ref>Makoto Watanabe, ''Jōmon Period Knowledge: Archaeology Series 4'', Tokyo Bijutsu, ISBN 4-8087-0190-1, 1983, p.132.</ref>.
== Shinto Graves ==
Shinto graves follow structures similar to Buddhist ones but omit incense burning, requiring no [[incense burners]]. Instead, an [[eight-legged stand]] (八足台) is used to offer [[sakaki]] branches.
Gravestones are tall, slender rectangular pillars with pyramid-shaped tops, symbolizing the [[Ame no Murakumo no Tsurugi]], one of the [[Three Sacred Treasures]]. Gravestones are inscribed with "○○ Family Okutsuki" or "○○ Family Okutsushiro." If no gravestone exists, the grave marker is inscribed with "○○ Elder (刀自) Spirit Okutsuki." In Shinto, there are no [[posthumous names]]; instead, the deceased's name is followed by "no Mitama," "no Mikoto," "Reisō," or "Reii."
[[Shrines]] typically do not own cemeteries. To establish a Shinto grave, families must purchase plots in public or private cemeteries.
Military personnel of the [[Empire of Japan]] were often enshrined in Shinto graves separate from their family's ancestral plots. During [[World War II]], sunny locations were allocated for the war dead<ref>Hideyoshi Ukai, ''Temple Disappearance,'' Nikkei BP, ISBN 978-4822279172, p.205.</ref>. Gravestones for officers sometimes included their branch (Army or Navy) and final rank before their names.

{{Cite sources clearly|date=August 28, 2017, 05:07 (UTC)}} '''Okutsuki''' (奥都城) refers to [[ancient|upper antiquity]] burial sites and, by extension, Shinto-style graves. It is also the term inscribed on gravestones in the Shinto style. Alternate spellings include '''Okutsushiro''' (奥津城) and '''Okujo''' (奥城).
== Meaning of the Term ==
"都" and "津 (tsu)" are [[Man'yōgana]] characters representing the ancient [[case particle]] "tsu," which means "of." The character "都" is used for graves of priests and ujiko (shrine parishioners), while "津" is for general believers. However, if ancestors served as [[priests]] or [[ujiko]] officials, "都" may also be used. Additionally, regional practices may favor one character over the other.
"奥 (oku)" conveys "deep" or "to place." "城 (ki)" refers to a space enclosed by walls or shelves, as in [[Isawa Castle]]'s usage, and also implies "coffin" ([[柩]]). Together, the term signifies "a secluded boundary shielded from the outside" or "a place to place a coffin."
In historical texts, the term "Okutsuki" appears in the ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}'' and "Okutsusutae" (奥津棄戸) in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}''. Initially, it described funerary practices involving the abandonment of corpses<ref>Fujii Masao, ''Basic Knowledge of Buddhist Rites'', Kodansha, 1985, ISBN 4-06-201068-2, p.116.</ref>. Commoners' remains were often left exposed, as evidenced archaeologically in ancient Japan, where there are no burial structures for ordinary people<ref>''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡}}'', entry for February 29, 1261 (Kōchō 1): The shogunate forbade corpse abandonment, a practice common even among medieval commoners.</ref>. Terms such as "hafuru" in texts like ''{{ill|Iroha Jirui Shō|ja|伊呂波字類抄}}'', ''{{ill|Shinzan-shū|ja|秦山集}}'', and ''{{ill|The Tales of Ise|ja|伊勢物語}}'' similarly denote abandonment and share meanings with "Okutsuki"<!-- Fujii Masao, p.115 -->. Over time, Shinto-style graves evolved under the influence of later Shinto scholars. During the [[Jōmon period]], disposing of remains in shell mounds served as a ritual to send spirits to the afterlife<ref>Makoto Watanabe, ''Jōmon Period Knowledge: Archaeology Series 4'', Tokyo Bijutsu, ISBN 4-8087-0190-1, 1983, p.132.</ref>.
== Shinto Graves ==
Shinto graves follow structures similar to Buddhist ones but omit incense burning, requiring no [[incense burners]]. Instead, an [[eight-legged stand]] (八足台) is used to offer [[sakaki]] branches.
Gravestones are tall, slender rectangular pillars with pyramid-shaped tops, symbolizing the [[Ame no Murakumo no Tsurugi]], one of the [[Three Sacred Treasures]]. Gravestones are inscribed with "○○ Family Okutsuki" or "○○ Family Okutsushiro." If no gravestone exists, the grave marker is inscribed with "○○ Elder (刀自) Spirit Okutsuki." In Shinto, there are no [[posthumous names]]; instead, the deceased's name is followed by "no Mitama," "no Mikoto," "Reisō," or "Reii."
[[Shrines]] typically do not own cemeteries. To establish a Shinto grave, families must purchase plots in public or private cemeteries.
Military personnel of the [[Empire of Japan]] were often enshrined in Shinto graves separate from their family's ancestral plots. During [[World War II]], sunny locations were allocated for the war dead<ref>Hideyoshi Ukai, ''Temple Disappearance,'' Nikkei BP, ISBN 978-4822279172, p.205.</ref>. Gravestones for officers sometimes included their branch (Army or Navy) and final rank before their names.

Terms such as "hafuru" in texts like ''{{ill|Iroha Jirui Shō|ja|伊呂波字類抄|12=draft}}'', ''{{ill|Shinzan-shū|ja|秦山集|12=draft}}'', and ''{{ill|The Tales of Ise|ja|伊勢物語|12=draft}}'' similarly denote abandonment and share meanings with "Okutsuki"<!-- Fujii Masao, p.115 -->. Over time, Shinto-style graves evolved under the influence of later Shinto scholars. During the [[Jōmon period]], disposing of remains in shell mounds served as a ritual to send spirits to the afterlife<ref>Makoto Watanabe, ''Jōmon Period Knowledge: Archaeology Series 4'', Tokyo Bijutsu, ISBN 4-8087-0190-1, 1983, p.132.</ref>.
== Shinto Graves ==
Shinto graves follow structures similar to Buddhist ones but omit incense burning, requiring no [[incense burners]]. Instead, an [[eight-legged stand]] (八足台) is used to offer [[sakaki]] branches.
Gravestones are tall, slender rectangular pillars with pyramid-shaped tops, symbolizing the [[Ame no Murakumo no Tsurugi]], one of the [[Three Sacred Treasures]]. Gravestones are inscribed with "○○ Family Okutsuki" or "○○ Family Okutsushiro." If no gravestone exists, the grave marker is inscribed with "○○ Elder (刀自) Spirit Okutsuki." In Shinto, there are no [[posthumous names]]; instead, the deceased's name is followed by "no Mitama," "no Mikoto," "Reisō," or "Reii."
[[Shrines]] typically do not own cemeteries. To establish a Shinto grave, families must purchase plots in public or private cemeteries.
Military personnel of the [[Empire of Japan]] were often enshrined in Shinto graves separate from their family's ancestral plots. During [[World War II]], sunny locations were allocated for the war dead<ref>Hideyoshi Ukai, ''Temple Disappearance,'' Nikkei BP, ISBN 978-4822279172, p.205.</ref>. Gravestones for officers sometimes included their branch (Army or Navy) and final rank before their names.

{{Cite sources clearly|date=August 28, 2017, 05:07 (UTC)}} '''Okutsuki''' (奥都城) refers to [[ancient|upper antiquity]] burial sites and, by extension, Shinto-style graves. It is also the term inscribed on gravestones in the Shinto style. Alternate spellings include '''Okutsushiro''' (奥津城) and '''Okujo''' (奥城).
== Meaning of the Term ==
"都" and "津 (tsu)" are [[Man'yōgana]] characters representing the ancient [[case particle]] "tsu," which means "of." The character "都" is used for graves of priests and ujiko (shrine parishioners), while "津" is for general believers. However, if ancestors served as [[priests]] or [[ujiko]] officials, "都" may also be used. Additionally, regional practices may favor one character over the other.
"奥 (oku)" conveys "deep" or "to place." "城 (ki)" refers to a space enclosed by walls or shelves, as in [[Isawa Castle]]'s usage, and also implies "coffin" ([[柩]]). Together, the term signifies "a secluded boundary shielded from the outside" or "a place to place a coffin."
In historical texts, the term "Okutsuki" appears in the ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集|12=draft}}'' and "Okutsusutae" (奥津棄戸) in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}''. Initially, it described funerary practices involving the abandonment of corpses<ref>Fujii Masao, ''Basic Knowledge of Buddhist Rites'', Kodansha, 1985, ISBN 4-06-201068-2, p.116.</ref>. Commoners' remains were often left exposed, as evidenced archaeologically in ancient Japan, where there are no burial structures for ordinary people<ref>''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡|12=draft}}'', entry for February 29, 1261 (Kōchō 1): The shogunate forbade corpse abandonment, a practice common even among medieval commoners.</ref>. Terms such as "hafuru" in texts like ''{{ill|Iroha Jirui Shō|ja|伊呂波字類抄|12=draft}}'', ''{{ill|Shinzan-shū|ja|秦山集|12=draft}}'', and ''{{ill|The Tales of Ise|ja|伊勢物語|12=draft}}'' similarly denote abandonment and share meanings with "Okutsuki"<!-- Fujii Masao, p.115 -->. Over time, Shinto-style graves evolved under the influence of later Shinto scholars. During the [[Jōmon period]], disposing of remains in shell mounds served as a ritual to send spirits to the afterlife<ref>Makoto Watanabe, ''Jōmon Period Knowledge: Archaeology Series 4'', Tokyo Bijutsu, ISBN 4-8087-0190-1, 1983, p.132.</ref>.
== Shinto Graves ==
Shinto graves follow structures similar to Buddhist ones but omit incense burning, requiring no [[incense burners]]. Instead, an [[eight-legged stand]] (八足台) is used to offer [[sakaki]] branches.
Gravestones are tall, slender rectangular pillars with pyramid-shaped tops, symbolizing the [[Ame no Murakumo no Tsurugi]], one of the [[Three Sacred Treasures]]. Gravestones are inscribed with "○○ Family Okutsuki" or "○○ Family Okutsushiro." If no gravestone exists, the grave marker is inscribed with "○○ Elder (刀自) Spirit Okutsuki." In Shinto, there are no [[posthumous names]]; instead, the deceased's name is followed by "no Mitama," "no Mikoto," "Reisō," or "Reii."
[[Shrines]] typically do not own cemeteries. To establish a Shinto grave, families must purchase plots in public or private cemeteries.
Military personnel of the [[Empire of Japan]] were often enshrined in Shinto graves separate from their family's ancestral plots. During [[World War II]], sunny locations were allocated for the war dead<ref>Hideyoshi Ukai, ''Temple Disappearance,'' Nikkei BP, ISBN 978-4822279172, p.205.</ref>. Gravestones for officers sometimes included their branch (Army or Navy) and final rank before their names.
== References == {{reflist}}

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 05:07 (UTC)}} '''奥都城'''（おくつき）とは、[[古代|上代]]の[[墓]]のこと。またそこから[[神道]]式の墓のこと。神道式の[[墓石]]に刻まれる文字でもある。'''奥津城'''、'''奥城'''とも書く。
== 言葉の意味 == 「都・津（つ）」は、上代の[[格助詞]]「[[つ (格助詞)|つ]]」に当てた[[万葉仮名]]で、「～の」の意味になる。「都」は、神官・氏子などを勤めた人の墓に使われる漢字で、「津」は一般信徒の墓に使われる。ただし、先祖に[[神官]]、[[氏子]]の[[役]]に従事した人がいる場合には「都」が使われることがある。またこれとは別に、地域により、どちらかの文字が広く用いられることもあり、一概ではない。
「奥（おく）」とは、奥深い意の「奥」や「置く」を意味するといわれる。「城（き）」は、古代の「[[胆沢城]]」の「城」の用例にみるように棚・壁などで四辺を取り囲んだ一郭の場所をいい、また「[[柩]]（ひつぎ）」の意味もあるとされる。全体の意味としては、「奥深い所にあって外部から遮られた境域」ということであり、また「柩を置く場所」の意となる。
文献上の例として、『[[万葉集]]』に「奥都城」、『[[日本書紀]]』神代巻に「奥津棄戸（おくつ すたへ）」と記されている。本来の意は、死体遺棄による葬法を表しているものであり<ref>藤井正雄 『仏事の基礎知識』 講談社 初版1985年 ISBN 4-06-201068-2 p.116参考</ref>、一般民衆の死体が遺棄されていた事による（考古学上においても、古代日本において一般人は墓を築いた形跡はなく、遺棄された状態である<ref>『[[吾妻鏡]]』[[弘長]]元年（[[1261年]]）2月29日条には、幕府が関東諸侯において、「～死屍（しし）を路地に弃（す）つる事を禁制すべし」と定めている事からも、死体遺棄が中世前半の庶民にとっても一般的な行いであった事が分かる。</ref>）。『[[伊呂波字類抄]]』、『[[秦山集]]』、『[[伊勢物語]]』、『[[古事記伝]]』などの文献中に葬式の事を「はふる」と記しているが、これも遺棄を意味するものであり、奥都城と同様の意味であるとされる<!-- 藤井正雄 『仏事の基礎知識』p.115 -->（『古事記伝』での表記は「波夫里（はふり）」と記す）。神道墓の形成は、後世の神道家によって大成されたものである。なお、[[縄文時代]]に[[貝塚]]に遺骸を捨てる風習があるが、これは一説<!-- 河野広道説 参考は後述書 p.132. -->に、貝塚自体が全ての生物の霊魂を他界へ送り返すための祭の場であり<!-- 河野説 参考後述書 -->、単なるゴミ捨て場ではなく、埋葬場として用いられたとするものがある<ref>[[渡辺誠 (考古学者)|渡辺誠]] 『縄文時代の知識 考古学シリーズ4』 [[東京美術]] ISBN 4-8087-0190-1 1983年 p.132.[[河野広道]]が[[アイヌ]]の習俗をヒントに得た説で、このため、親子兄弟の遺骸を棄てたとする。</ref>。
== 神道墓 == 基本的な構成は、仏式と同じであるが、神道では[[焼香]]を行わないので、[[香炉]]は要らない。また[[玉串]]を奉げる為の[[八足台]]が要る。 墓石の形は、細長い[[角柱]]型で、頂上部は[[四角錐]]になっている。この形は[[三種の神器]]の一つ[[天叢雲剣]]を表しているとされる。
墓石には「○○家奥都城」或いは「○○家奥津城」と刻む。墓石がない場合は墓標に「○○大人（刀自）命奥都城」と書く。神道では[[戒名]]はなく、姓名の下に、之霊・命・命霊・霊位などを付ける。
[[神社]]では通常[[墓地]]を所有していない。神式でお墓を建立する場合は公営や民営の[[霊園]]の[[墓地]]を買わなければならない。
大日本帝国の軍人は先祖と同じ墓には入らず神道墓に祀られることが多かった。特に第二次大戦中の戦死者は日当たりの良い場所が割り当てられた<ref>鵜飼秀徳 (著) 『寺院消滅』 [[日経BP社]] ISBN 978-4822279172 p205</ref>。将校の墓石は姓名の前に所属（陸軍か海軍）と最終階級が掘られていることもある。
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
== 関連項目 == *[[御霊舎]] *[[生祀]] *[[神葬祭]] {{神道 横}} {{Shinto-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:おくつき}} [[Category:墓]] [[Category:神道]]

Gravestones are inscribed with "○○ Family Okutsuki" or "○○ Family Okutsushiro." If no gravestone exists, the grave marker is inscribed with "○○ Elder (刀自) Spirit Okutsuki." In Shinto, there are no [[posthumous names]]; instead, the deceased's name is followed by "no Mitama," "no Mikoto," "Reisō," or "Reii."
[[Shrines]] typically do not own cemeteries. To establish a Shinto grave, families must purchase plots in public or private cemeteries.
Military personnel of the [[Empire of Japan]] were often enshrined in Shinto graves separate from their family's ancestral plots. During [[World War II]], sunny locations were allocated for the war dead<ref>Hideyoshi Ukai, ''Temple Disappearance,'' Nikkei BP, ISBN 978-4822279172, p.205.</ref>. Gravestones for officers sometimes included their branch (Army or Navy) and final Rank before their names.

{{Cite sources clearly|date=August 28, 2017, 05:07 (UTC)}} '''Okutsuki''' (奥都城) refers to [[ancient|upper antiquity]] burial sites and, by extension, Shinto-style graves. It is also the term inscribed on gravestones in the Shinto style. Alternate spellings include '''Okutsushiro''' (奥津城) and '''Okujo''' (奥城).
== Meaning of the Term ==
"都" and "津 (tsu)" are [[Man'yōgana]] characters representing the ancient [[case particle]] "tsu," which means "of." The character "都" is used for graves of priests and ujiko (shrine parishioners), while "津" is for general believers. However, if ancestors served as [[priests]] or [[ujiko]] officials, "都" may also be used. Additionally, regional practices may favor one character over the other.
"奥 (oku)" conveys "deep" or "to place." "城 (ki)" refers to a space enclosed by walls or shelves, as in [[Isawa Castle]]'s usage, and also implies "coffin" ([[柩]]). Together, the term signifies "a secluded boundary shielded from the outside" or "a place to place a coffin."
In historical texts, the term "Okutsuki" appears in the ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集|qq=draft}}'' and "Okutsusutae" (奥津棄戸) in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft}}''. Initially, it described funerary practices involving the abandonment of corpses<ref>Fujii Masao, ''Basic Knowledge of Buddhist Rites'', Kodansha, 1985, ISBN 4-06-201068-2, p.116.</ref>. Commoners' remains were often left exposed, as evidenced archaeologically in ancient Japan, where there are no burial structures for ordinary people<ref>''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|ja|吾妻鏡|qq=draft}}'', entry for February 29, 1261 (Kōchō 1): The shogunate forbade corpse abandonment, a practice common even among medieval commoners.</ref>. Terms such as "hafuru" in texts like ''{{ill|Iroha Jirui Shō|ja|伊呂波字類抄|qq=draft}}'', ''{{ill|Shinzan-shū|ja|秦山集|qq=draft}}'', and ''{{ill|The Tales of Ise|ja|伊勢物語|qq=draft}}'' similarly denote abandonment and share meanings with "Okutsuki". Over time, Shinto-style graves evolved under the influence of later Shinto scholars. During the [[Jōmon period]], disposing of remains in shell mounds served as a ritual to send spirits to the afterlife<ref>Makoto Watanabe, ''Jōmon Period Knowledge: Archaeology Series 4'', Tokyo Bijutsu, ISBN 4-8087-0190-1, 1983, p.132.</ref>.
== Shinto Graves ==
Shinto graves follow structures similar to Buddhist ones but omit incense burning, requiring no [[incense burners]]. Instead, an [[eight-legged stand]] (八足台) is used to offer [[sakaki]] branches.
Gravestones are tall, slender rectangular pillars with pyramid-shaped tops, symbolizing the [[Ame no Murakumo no Tsurugi]], one of the [[Three Sacred Treasures]]. Gravestones are inscribed with "○○ Family Okutsuki" or "○○ Family Okutsushiro." If no gravestone exists, the grave marker is inscribed with "○○ Elder (刀自) Spirit Okutsuki." In Shinto, there are no [[posthumous names]]; instead, the deceased's name is followed by "no Mitama," "no Mikoto," "Reisō," or "Reii."
[[Shrines]] typically do not own cemeteries. To establish a Shinto grave, families must purchase plots in public or private cemeteries.
Military personnel of the [[Empire of Japan]] were often enshrined in Shinto graves separate from their family's ancestral plots. During [[World War II]], sunny locations were allocated for the war dead<ref>Hideyoshi Ukai, ''Temple Disappearance,'' Nikkei BP, ISBN 978-4822279172, p.205.</ref>. Gravestones for officers sometimes included their branch (Army or Navy) and final Rank before their names.

{{Unreferenced clearly|date=August 28, 2017, 05:07 (UTC)}}
'''Okutsuki''' (奥都城) refers to [[:en:Ancient history|upper antiquity]] burial sites and, by extension, Shinto-style graves. It is also the term inscribed on gravestones in the Shinto style. Alternate spellings include '''Okutsushiro''' (奥津城) and '''Okujo''' (奥城).

== Meaning of the Term ==

"都" and "津 (tsu)" are [[:en:Man'yōgana|Man'yōgana]] characters representing the ancient [[case particle]] "tsu," which means "of." The character "都" is used for graves of priests and ujiko (shrine parishioners), while "津" is for general believers. However, if ancestors served as [[:en:priests|priests]] or [[:en:Ujiko|Ujiko]] officials, "都" may also be used. Additionally, regional practices may favor one character over the other.

"奥 (oku)" conveys "deep" or "to place." "城 (ki)" refers to a space enclosed by walls or shelves, as in [[:en:Isawa Castle|Isawa Castle]]'s usage, and also implies "coffin" ([[柩]]). Together, the term signifies "a secluded boundary shielded from the outside" or "a place to place a coffin."

In historical texts, the term "Okutsuki" appears in the ''[[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]]'' and "Okutsusutae" (奥津棄戸) in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]''. Initially, it described funerary practices involving the abandonment of corpses<ref>Fujii Masao, ''Basic Knowledge of Buddhist Rites'', Kodansha, 1985, ISBN 4-06-201068-2, p.116.</ref>. Commoners' remains were often left exposed, as evidenced archaeologically in ancient Japan, where there are no burial structures for ordinary people<ref>''[[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]]'', entry for February 29, 1261 (Kōchō 1): The shogunate forbade corpse abandonment, a practice common even among medieval commoners.</ref>. Terms such as "hafuru" in texts like ''{{ill|Iroha Jirui Shō|ja|伊呂波字類抄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iroha Jirui Shō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 色葉字類抄|1=Iroha Jiruishō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11614525}}'', ''{{ill|Shinzan-shū|ja|秦山集|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinzan-shū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', and ''[[:en:The Tales of Ise|The Tales of Ise]]'' similarly denote abandonment and share meanings with "Okutsuki". Over time, Shinto-style graves evolved under the influence of later Shinto scholars. During the [[Jōmon period]], disposing of remains in shell mounds served as a ritual to send spirits to the afterlife<ref>Makoto Watanabe, ''Jōmon Period Knowledge: Archaeology Series 4'', Tokyo Bijutsu, ISBN 4-8087-0190-1, 1983, p.132.</ref>.

== Shinto Graves ==

Shinto graves follow structures similar to Buddhist ones but omit incense burning, requiring no [[incense burners]]. Instead, an [[eight-legged stand]] (八足台) is used to offer [[:en:Cleyera japonica|Cleyera japonica]] branches.

Gravestones are tall, slender rectangular pillars with pyramid-shaped tops, symbolizing the [[Ame no Murakumo no Tsurugi]], one of the [[:en:Three Sacred Treasures|Three Sacred Treasures]]. Gravestones are inscribed with "○○ Family Okutsuki" or "○○ Family Okutsushiro." If no gravestone exists, the grave marker is inscribed with "○○ Elder (刀自) Spirit Okutsuki." In Shinto, there are no [[:en:Posthumous names|Posthumous names]]; instead, the deceased's name is followed by "no Mitama," "no Mikoto," "Reisō," or "Reii."

[[:en:Shrines|Shrines]] typically do not own cemeteries. To establish a Shinto grave, families must purchase plots in public or private cemeteries.

Military personnel of the [[:en:Empire of Japan|Empire of Japan]] were often enshrined in Shinto graves separate from their family's ancestral plots. During [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], sunny locations were allocated for the war dead<ref>Hideyoshi Ukai, ''Temple Disappearance,'' Nikkei BP, ISBN 978-4822279172, p.205.</ref>. Gravestones for officers sometimes included their branch (Army or Navy) and final Rank before their names.

"都" and "津 (tsu)" are [[:en:Man'yōgana|Man'yōgana]] characters representing the ancient [[case particle]] "tsu," which means "of." The character "都" is used for graves of priests and ujiko (shrine parishioners), while "津" is for general believers. However, if ancestors served as [[:en:priests|priests]] or [[:en:Ujiko|Ujiko]] officials, "都" may also be used. Additionally, regional practices may favor one character over the other.

"奥 (oku)" conveys "deep" or "to place." "城 (ki)" refers to a space enclosed by walls or shelves, as in [[:en:Isawa Castle|Isawa Castle]]'s usage, and also implies "coffin" ([[柩]]). Together, the term signifies "a secluded boundary shielded from the outside" or "a place to place a coffin."

In historical texts, the term "Okutsuki" appears in the ''[[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]]'' and "Okutsusutae" (奥津棄戸) in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]''. Initially, it described funerary practices involving the abandonment of corpses<ref>Fujii Masao, ''Basic Knowledge of Buddhist Rites'', Kodansha, 1985, ISBN 4-06-201068-2, p.116.</ref>. Commoners' remains were often left exposed, as evidenced archaeologically in ancient Japan, where there are no burial structures for ordinary people<ref>''[[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]]'', entry for February 29, 1261 (Kōchō 1): The shogunate forbade corpse abandonment, a practice common even among medieval commoners.</ref>. Terms such as "hafuru" in texts like ''{{ill|Iroha Jirui Shō|ja|伊呂波字類抄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iroha Jirui Shō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 色葉字類抄|1=Iroha Jiruishō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11614525}}'', ''{{ill|Shinzan-shū|ja|秦山集|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinzan-shū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', and ''[[:en:The Tales of Ise|The Tales of Ise]]'' similarly denote abandonment and share meanings with "Okutsuki". Over time, Shinto-style graves evolved under the influence of later Shinto scholars. During the [[Jōmon period]], disposing of remains in shell mounds served as a ritual to send spirits to the afterlife<ref>Makoto Watanabe, ''Jōmon Period Knowledge: Archaeology Series 4'', Tokyo Bijutsu, ISBN 4-8087-0190-1, 1983, p.132.</ref>.

== Shinto Graves ==

Shinto graves follow structures similar to Buddhist ones but omit incense burning, requiring no [[incense burners]]. Instead, an [[eight-legged stand]] (八足台) is used to offer [[:en:Cleyera japonica|Cleyera japonica]] branches.

Gravestones are tall, slender rectangular pillars with pyramid-shaped tops, symbolizing the [[Ame no Murakumo no Tsurugi]], one of the [[:en:Three Sacred Treasures|Three Sacred Treasures]]. Gravestones are inscribed with "○○ Family Okutsuki" or "○○ Family Okutsushiro." If no gravestone exists, the grave marker is inscribed with "○○ Elder (刀自) Spirit Okutsuki." In Shinto, there are no [[:en:Posthumous names|Posthumous names]]; instead, the deceased's name is followed by "no Mitama," "no Mikoto," "Reisō," or "Reii."

[[:en:Shrines|Shrines]] typically do not own cemeteries. To establish a Shinto grave, families must purchase plots in public or private cemeteries.

Military personnel of the [[:en:Empire of Japan|Empire of Japan]] were often enshrined in Shinto graves separate from their family's ancestral plots. During [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], sunny locations were allocated for the war dead<ref>Hideyoshi Ukai, ''Temple Disappearance,'' Nikkei BP, ISBN 978-4822279172, p.205.</ref>. Gravestones for officers sometimes included their branch (Army or Navy) and final Rank before their names.

Gravestones are tall, slender rectangular pillars with pyramid-shaped tops, symbolizing the [[Ame no Murakumo no Tsurugi]], one of the [[:en:Three Sacred Treasures|Three Sacred Treasures]]. Gravestones are inscribed with "○○ Family Okutsuki" or "○○ Family Okutsushiro." If no gravestone exists, the grave marker is inscribed with "○○ Elder (刀自) Spirit Okutsuki." In Shinto, there are no [[:en:Posthumous names|Posthumous names]]; instead, the deceased's name is followed by "no Mitama," "no Mikoto," "Reisō," or "Reii."

[[:en:Shrines|Shrines]] typically do not own cemeteries. To establish a Shinto grave, families must purchase plots in public or private cemeteries.

During [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], sunny locations were allocated for the war dead<ref>Hideyoshi Ukai, ''Temple Disappearance,'' Nikkei BP, ISBN 978-4822279172, p.205.</ref>. Gravestones for officers sometimes included their branch (Army or Navy) and final Rank before their names.

Commoners' remains were often left exposed, as evidenced archaeologically in ancient Japan, where there are no burial structures for ordinary people<ref>''[[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]]'', entry for February 29, 1261 (Kōchō 1): The shogunate forbade corpse abandonment, a practice common even among medieval commoners.</ref>. Terms such as "hafuru" in texts like ''{{ill|Iroha Jirui Shō|ja|伊呂波字類抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iroha Jirui Shō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 色葉字類抄|1=Iroha Jiruishō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11614525}}'', ''{{ill|Shinzan-shū|ja|秦山集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinzan-shū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', and ''[[:en:The Tales of Ise|The Tales of Ise]]'' similarly denote abandonment and share meanings with "Okutsuki". Over time, Shinto-style graves evolved under the influence of later Shinto scholars. During the [[Jōmon period]], disposing of remains in shell mounds served as a ritual to send spirits to the afterlife<ref>Makoto Watanabe, ''Jōmon Period Knowledge: Archaeology Series 4'', Tokyo Bijutsu, ISBN 4-8087-0190-1, 1983, p.132.</ref>.
== Shinto Graves ==
Shinto graves follow structures similar to Buddhist ones but omit incense burning, requiring no [[incense burners]]. Instead, an [[eight-legged stand]] (八足台) is used to offer [[:en:Cleyera japonica|Cleyera japonica]] branches.
Gravestones are tall, slender rectangular pillars with pyramid-shaped tops, symbolizing the [[Ame no Murakumo no Tsurugi]], one of the [[:en:Three Sacred Treasures|Three Sacred Treasures]]. Gravestones are inscribed with "○○ Family Okutsuki" or "○○ Family Okutsushiro." If no gravestone exists, the grave marker is inscribed with "○○ Elder (刀自) Spirit Okutsuki." In Shinto, there are no [[:en:Posthumous names|Posthumous names]]; instead, the deceased's name is followed by "no Mitama," "no Mikoto," "Reisō," or "Reii."
[[:en:Shrines|Shrines]] typically do not own cemeteries. To establish a Shinto grave, families must purchase plots in public or private cemeteries.
Military personnel of the [[:en:Empire of Japan|Empire of Japan]] were often enshrined in Shinto graves separate from their family's ancestral plots. During [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], sunny locations were allocated for the war dead<ref>Hideyoshi Ukai, ''Temple Disappearance,'' Nikkei BP, ISBN 978-4822279172, p.205.</ref>. Gravestones for officers sometimes included their branch (Army or Navy) and final Rank before their names.

It is also the term inscribed on gravestones in the Shinto style. Alternate spellings include '''Okutsushiro''' (奥津城) and '''Okujo''' (奥城).
