"都" and "津 (tsu)" are [[:en:Man'yōgana|Man'yōgana]] characters representing the ancient [[case particle]] "tsu," which means "of." The character "都" is used for graves of priests and ujiko (shrine parishioners), while "津" is for general believers. However, if ancestors served as [[:en:priests|priests]] or [[:en:Ujiko|Ujiko]] officials, "都" may also be used. Additionally, regional practices may favor one character over the other.
"奥 (oku)" conveys "deep" or "to place." "城 (ki)" refers to a space enclosed by walls or shelves, as in [[:en:Isawa Castle|Isawa Castle]]'s usage, and also implies "coffin" ([[柩]]). Together, the term signifies "a secluded boundary shielded from the outside" or "a place to place a coffin."
In historical texts, the term "Okutsuki" appears in the ''[[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]]'' and "Okutsusutae" (奥津棄戸) in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]''. Commoners' remains were often left exposed, as evidenced archaeologically in ancient Japan, where there are no burial structures for ordinary people<ref>''[[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]]'', entry for February 29, 1261 (Kōchō 1): The shogunate forbade corpse abandonment, a practice common even among medieval commoners.</ref>. Over time, Shinto-style graves evolved under the influence of later Shinto scholars. During the [[Jōmon period]], disposing of remains in shell mounds served as a ritual to send spirits to the afterlife<ref>Makoto Watanabe, ''Jōmon Period Knowledge: Archaeology Series 4'', Tokyo Bijutsu, ISBN 4-8087-0190-1, 1983, p.132.</ref>.

Shinto graves follow structures similar to Buddhist ones but omit incense burning, requiring no [[incense burners]]. Instead, an [[eight-legged stand]] (八足台) is used to offer [[:en:Cleyera japonica|Cleyera japonica]] branches.
Gravestones are tall, slender rectangular pillars with pyramid-shaped tops, symbolizing the [[Ame no Murakumo no Tsurugi]], one of the [[:en:Three Sacred Treasures|Three Sacred Treasures]]. Gravestones are inscribed with "○○ Family Okutsuki" or "○○ Family Okutsushiro." If no gravestone exists, the grave marker is inscribed with "○○ Elder (刀自) Spirit Okutsuki." In Shinto, there are no [[:en:Posthumous names|Posthumous names]]; instead, the deceased's name is followed by "no Mitama," "no Mikoto," "Reisō," or "Reii."
[[:en:Shrines|Shrines]] typically do not own cemeteries. To establish a Shinto grave, families must purchase plots in public or private cemeteries.
Military personnel of the [[:en:Empire of Japan|Empire of Japan]] were often enshrined in Shinto graves separate from their family's ancestral plots. During [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], sunny locations were allocated for the war dead<ref>Hideyoshi Ukai, ''Temple Disappearance,'' Nikkei BP, ISBN 978-4822279172, p.205.</ref>. Gravestones for officers sometimes included their branch (Army or Navy) and final Rank before their names.

Terms such as "hafuru" in texts like ''{{ill|Iroha Jirui Shō|ja|伊呂波字類抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iroha Jirui Shō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 色葉字類抄|1=Iroha Jiruishō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11614525}}'', ''{{ill|Shinzan-shū|ja|秦山集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinzan-shū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', and ''[[:en:The Tales of Ise|The Tales of Ise]]'' similarly denote abandonment and share meanings with "Okutsuki". Over time, Shinto-style graves evolved under the influence of later Shinto scholars. During the [[Jōmon period]], disposing of remains in shell mounds served as a ritual to send spirits to the afterlife<ref>Makoto Watanabe, ''Jōmon Period Knowledge: Archaeology Series 4'', Tokyo Bijutsu, ISBN 4-8087-0190-1, 1983, p.132.</ref>.
== Shinto Graves ==
Shinto graves follow structures similar to Buddhist ones but omit incense burning, requiring no [[incense burners]]. Instead, an [[eight-legged stand]] (八足台) is used to offer [[:en:Cleyera japonica|Cleyera japonica]] branches.
Gravestones are tall, slender rectangular pillars with pyramid-shaped tops, symbolizing the [[Ame no Murakumo no Tsurugi]], one of the [[:en:Three Sacred Treasures|Three Sacred Treasures]]. Gravestones are inscribed with "○○ Family Okutsuki" or "○○ Family Okutsushiro." If no gravestone exists, the grave marker is inscribed with "○○ Elder (刀自) Spirit Okutsuki." In Shinto, there are no [[:en:Posthumous names|Posthumous names]]; instead, the deceased's name is followed by "no Mitama," "no Mikoto," "Reisō," or "Reii."
[[:en:Shrines|Shrines]] typically do not own cemeteries. To establish a Shinto grave, families must purchase plots in public or private cemeteries.
Military personnel of the [[:en:Empire of Japan|Empire of Japan]] were often enshrined in Shinto graves separate from their family's ancestral plots. During [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], sunny locations were allocated for the war dead<ref>Hideyoshi Ukai, ''Temple Disappearance,'' Nikkei BP, ISBN 978-4822279172, p.205.</ref>. Gravestones for officers sometimes included their branch (Army or Navy) and final Rank before their names.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:奥都城]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:奥都城]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:奥都城]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11446137]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced clearly|date=August 28, 2017, 05:07 (UTC)}} '''Okutsuki''' (奥都城) refers to [[:en:Ancient history|upper antiquity]] burial sites and, by extension, Shinto-style graves. It is also the term inscribed on gravestones in the Shinto style. Alternate spellings include '''Okutsushiro''' (奥津城) and '''Okujo''' (奥城).
== Meaning of the Term ==
"都" and "津 (tsu)" are [[:en:Man'yōgana|Man'yōgana]] characters representing the ancient [[case particle]] "tsu," which means "of." The character "都" is used for graves of priests and ujiko (shrine parishioners), while "津" is for general believers. However, if ancestors served as [[:en:priests|priests]] or [[:en:Ujiko|Ujiko]] officials, "都" may also be used. Additionally, regional practices may favor one character over the other.
"奥 (oku)" conveys "deep" or "to place." "城 (ki)" refers to a space enclosed by walls or shelves, as in [[:en:Isawa Castle|Isawa Castle]]'s usage, and also implies "coffin" ([[柩]]). Together, the term signifies "a secluded boundary shielded from the outside" or "a place to place a coffin."
In historical texts, the term "Okutsuki" appears in the ''[[:en:Man'yōshū|Man'yōshū]]'' and "Okutsusutae" (奥津棄戸) in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]''. Initially, it described funerary practices involving the abandonment of corpses<ref>Fujii Masao, ''Basic Knowledge of Buddhist Rites'', Kodansha, 1985, ISBN 4-06-201068-2, p.116.</ref>. Commoners' remains were often left exposed, as evidenced archaeologically in ancient Japan, where there are no burial structures for ordinary people<ref>''[[:en:Azuma Kagami|Azuma Kagami]]'', entry for February 29, 1261 (Kōchō 1): The shogunate forbade corpse abandonment, a practice common even among medieval commoners.</ref>. Over time, Shinto-style graves evolved under the influence of later Shinto scholars. During the [[Jōmon period]], disposing of remains in shell mounds served as a ritual to send spirits to the afterlife<ref>Makoto Watanabe, ''Jōmon Period Knowledge: Archaeology Series 4'', Tokyo Bijutsu, ISBN 4-8087-0190-1, 1983, p.132.</ref>.
== Shinto Graves ==
Shinto graves follow structures similar to Buddhist ones but omit incense burning, requiring no [[incense burners]]. Instead, an [[eight-legged stand]] (八足台) is used to offer [[:en:Cleyera japonica|Cleyera japonica]] branches.
Gravestones are tall, slender rectangular pillars with pyramid-shaped tops, symbolizing the [[Ame no Murakumo no Tsurugi]], one of the [[:en:Three Sacred Treasures|Three Sacred Treasures]]. Gravestones are inscribed with "○○ Family Okutsuki" or "○○ Family Okutsushiro." If no gravestone exists, the grave marker is inscribed with "○○ Elder (刀自) Spirit Okutsuki." In Shinto, there are no [[:en:Posthumous names|Posthumous names]]; instead, the deceased's name is followed by "no Mitama," "no Mikoto," "Reisō," or "Reii."
[[:en:Shrines|Shrines]] typically do not own cemeteries. To establish a Shinto grave, families must purchase plots in public or private cemeteries.
Military personnel of the [[:en:Empire of Japan|Empire of Japan]] were often enshrined in Shinto graves separate from their family's ancestral plots. During [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], sunny locations were allocated for the war dead<ref>Hideyoshi Ukai, ''Temple Disappearance,'' Nikkei BP, ISBN 978-4822279172, p.205.</ref>. Gravestones for officers sometimes included their branch (Army or Navy) and final Rank before their names.

In historical texts, the term "Okutsuki" appears in the ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|WD=Q834321|lt=Man'yōshū}}'' and "Okutsusutae" (奥津棄戸) in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}''. Initially, it described funerary practices involving the abandonment of corpses<ref>Fujii Masao, ''Basic Knowledge of Buddhist Rites'', Kodansha, 1985, ISBN 4-06-201068-2, p.116.</ref>. Commoners' remains were often left exposed, as evidenced archaeologically in ancient Japan, where there are no burial structures for ordinary people<ref>''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|en|Azuma Kagami|WD=Q1042324|lt=Azuma Kagami}}'', entry for February 29, 1261 (Kōchō 1): The shogunate forbade corpse abandonment, a practice common even among medieval commoners.</ref>. Terms such as "hafuru" in texts like ''{{ill|Iroha Jirui Shō|ja|伊呂波字類抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iroha Jirui Shō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 色葉字類抄|1=Iroha Jiruishō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11614525}}'', ''{{ill|Shinzan-shū|ja|秦山集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinzan-shū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', and ''{{ill|The Tales of Ise|en|The Tales of Ise|WD=Q1539995|lt=The Tales of Ise}}'' similarly denote abandonment and share meanings with "Okutsuki". Over time, Shinto-style graves evolved under the influence of later Shinto scholars. During the [[Jōmon period]], disposing of remains in shell mounds served as a ritual to send spirits to the afterlife<ref>Makoto Watanabe, ''Jōmon Period Knowledge: Archaeology Series 4'', Tokyo Bijutsu, ISBN 4-8087-0190-1, 1983, p.132.</ref>.
== Shinto Graves ==
Shinto graves follow structures similar to Buddhist ones but omit incense burning, requiring no [[incense burners]]. Instead, an [[eight-legged stand]] (八足台) is used to offer {{ill|Cleyera japonica|en|Cleyera japonica|WD=Q775470|lt=Cleyera japonica}} branches.
Gravestones are tall, slender rectangular pillars with pyramid-shaped tops, symbolizing the [[Ame no Murakumo no Tsurugi]], one of the [[:en:Three Sacred Treasures|Three Sacred Treasures]]. Gravestones are inscribed with "○○ Family Okutsuki" or "○○ Family Okutsushiro." If no gravestone exists, the grave marker is inscribed with "○○ Elder (刀自) Spirit Okutsuki." In Shinto, there are no [[:en:Posthumous names|Posthumous names]]; instead, the deceased's name is followed by "no Mitama," "no Mikoto," "Reisō," or "Reii."
[[:en:Shrines|Shrines]] typically do not own cemeteries. To establish a Shinto grave, families must purchase plots in public or private cemeteries.
Military personnel of the {{ill|Empire of Japan|en|Empire of Japan|WD=Q188712|lt=Empire of Japan}} were often enshrined in Shinto graves separate from their family's ancestral plots. During {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War}}, sunny locations were allocated for the war dead<ref>Hideyoshi Ukai, ''Temple Disappearance,'' Nikkei BP, ISBN 978-4822279172, p.205.</ref>. Gravestones for officers sometimes included their branch (Army or Navy) and final Rank before their names.

It is also the term inscribed on gravestones in the Shinto style. Alternate spellings include '''Okutsushiro''' (奥津城) and '''Okujo''' (奥城).
== Meaning of the Term ==
"都" and "津 (tsu)" are {{ill|Man'yōgana|en|Man'yōgana|WD=Q862803|lt=Man'yōgana}} characters representing the ancient [[case particle]] "tsu," which means "of." The character "都" is used for graves of priests and ujiko (shrine parishioners), while "津" is for general believers. However, if ancestors served as [[:en:priests|priests]] or {{ill|Ujiko|en|Ujiko|WD=Q125562175|lt=Ujiko}} officials, "都" may also be used. Additionally, regional practices may favor one character over the other.
"奥 (oku)" conveys "deep" or "to place." "城 (ki)" refers to a space enclosed by walls or shelves, as in {{ill|Isawa Castle|en|Isawa Castle|WD=Q11611683|lt=Isawa Castle}}'s usage, and also implies "coffin" ([[柩]]). Together, the term signifies "a secluded boundary shielded from the outside" or "a place to place a coffin."
In historical texts, the term "Okutsuki" appears in the ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|WD=Q834321|lt=Man'yōshū}}'' and "Okutsusutae" (奥津棄戸) in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}''. Initially, it described funerary practices involving the abandonment of corpses<ref>Fujii Masao, ''Basic Knowledge of Buddhist Rites'', Kodansha, 1985, ISBN 4-06-201068-2, p.116.</ref>. Commoners' remains were often left exposed, as evidenced archaeologically in ancient Japan, where there are no burial structures for ordinary people<ref>''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|en|Azuma Kagami|WD=Q1042324|lt=Azuma Kagami}}'', entry for February 29, 1261 (Kōchō 1): The shogunate forbade corpse abandonment, a practice common even among medieval commoners.</ref>. Over time, Shinto-style graves evolved under the influence of later Shinto scholars. During the [[Jōmon period]], disposing of remains in shell mounds served as a ritual to send spirits to the afterlife<ref>Makoto Watanabe, ''Jōmon Period Knowledge: Archaeology Series 4'', Tokyo Bijutsu, ISBN 4-8087-0190-1, 1983, p.132.</ref>.
== Shinto Graves ==
Shinto graves follow structures similar to Buddhist ones but omit incense burning, requiring no [[incense burners]]. Instead, an [[eight-legged stand]] (八足台) is used to offer {{ill|Cleyera japonica|en|Cleyera japonica|WD=Q775470|lt=Cleyera japonica}} branches.
Gravestones are tall, slender rectangular pillars with pyramid-shaped tops, symbolizing the [[Ame no Murakumo no Tsurugi]], one of the [[:en:Three Sacred Treasures|Three Sacred Treasures]]. Gravestones are inscribed with "○○ Family Okutsuki" or "○○ Family Okutsushiro." If no gravestone exists, the grave marker is inscribed with "○○ Elder (刀自) Spirit Okutsuki." In Shinto, there are no [[:en:Posthumous names|Posthumous names]]; instead, the deceased's name is followed by "no Mitama," "no Mikoto," "Reisō," or "Reii."
[[:en:Shrines|Shrines]] typically do not own cemeteries. To establish a Shinto grave, families must purchase plots in public or private cemeteries.
Military personnel of the {{ill|Empire of Japan|en|Empire of Japan|WD=Q188712|lt=Empire of Japan}} were often enshrined in Shinto graves separate from their family's ancestral plots. During {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War}}, sunny locations were allocated for the war dead<ref>Hideyoshi Ukai, ''Temple Disappearance,'' Nikkei BP, ISBN 978-4822279172, p.205.</ref>. Gravestones for officers sometimes included their branch (Army or Navy) and final Rank before their names.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:奥都城]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:奥都城]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:奥都城]]
{{Unreferenced clearly|date=August 28, 2017, 05:07 (UTC)}} '''Okutsuki''' (奥都城) refers to {{ill|Ancient history|en|Ancient history|WD=Q41493|lt=upper antiquity|1=Ancient history}} burial sites and, by extension, Shinto-style graves. It is also the term inscribed on gravestones in the Shinto style. Alternate spellings include '''Okutsushiro''' (奥津城) and '''Okujo''' (奥城).
== Meaning of the Term ==
"都" and "津 (tsu)" are {{ill|Man'yōgana|en|Man'yōgana|WD=Q862803|lt=Man'yōgana|1=Man'yōgana}} characters representing the ancient [[case particle]] "tsu," which means "of." The character "都" is used for graves of priests and ujiko (shrine parishioners), while "津" is for general believers. However, if ancestors served as [[:en:priests|priests]] or {{ill|Ujiko|en|Ujiko|WD=Q125562175|lt=Ujiko|1=Ujiko}} officials, "都" may also be used. Additionally, regional practices may favor one character over the other.
"奥 (oku)" conveys "deep" or "to place." "城 (ki)" refers to a space enclosed by walls or shelves, as in {{ill|Isawa Castle|en|Isawa Castle|WD=Q11611683|lt=Isawa Castle|1=Isawa Castle}}'s usage, and also implies "coffin" ([[柩]]). Together, the term signifies "a secluded boundary shielded from the outside" or "a place to place a coffin."
In historical texts, the term "Okutsuki" appears in the ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|WD=Q834321|lt=Man'yōshū|1=Man'yōshū}}'' and "Okutsusutae" (奥津棄戸) in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}''. Initially, it described funerary practices involving the abandonment of corpses<ref>Fujii Masao, ''Basic Knowledge of Buddhist Rites'', Kodansha, 1985, ISBN 4-06-201068-2, p.116.</ref>. Commoners' remains were often left exposed, as evidenced archaeologically in ancient Japan, where there are no burial structures for ordinary people<ref>''{{ill|Azuma Kagami|en|Azuma Kagami|WD=Q1042324|lt=Azuma Kagami|1=Azuma Kagami}}'', entry for February 29, 1261 (Kōchō 1): The shogunate forbade corpse abandonment, a practice common even among medieval commoners.</ref>. Terms such as "hafuru" in texts like ''{{ill|Iroha Jirui Shō|ja|伊呂波字類抄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iroha Jirui Shō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 色葉字類抄|1=Iroha Jiruishō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11614525}}'', ''{{ill|Shinzan-shū|ja|秦山集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinzan-shū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', and ''{{ill|The Tales of Ise|en|The Tales of Ise|WD=Q1539995|lt=The Tales of Ise|1=The Tales of Ise}}'' similarly denote abandonment and share meanings with "Okutsuki". Over time, Shinto-style graves evolved under the influence of later Shinto scholars. During the [[Jōmon period]], disposing of remains in shell mounds served as a ritual to send spirits to the afterlife<ref>Makoto Watanabe, ''Jōmon Period Knowledge: Archaeology Series 4'', Tokyo Bijutsu, ISBN 4-8087-0190-1, 1983, p.132.</ref>.
== Shinto Graves ==
Shinto graves follow structures similar to Buddhist ones but omit incense burning, requiring no [[incense burners]]. Instead, an [[eight-legged stand]] (八足台) is used to offer {{ill|Cleyera japonica|en|Cleyera japonica|WD=Q775470|lt=Cleyera japonica|1=Cleyera japonica}} branches.
Gravestones are tall, slender rectangular pillars with pyramid-shaped tops, symbolizing the [[Ame no Murakumo no Tsurugi]], one of the [[:en:Three Sacred Treasures|Three Sacred Treasures]]. Gravestones are inscribed with "○○ Family Okutsuki" or "○○ Family Okutsushiro." If no gravestone exists, the grave marker is inscribed with "○○ Elder (刀自) Spirit Okutsuki." In Shinto, there are no [[:en:Posthumous names|Posthumous names]]; instead, the deceased's name is followed by "no Mitama," "no Mikoto," "Reisō," or "Reii."
[[:en:Shrines|Shrines]] typically do not own cemeteries. To establish a Shinto grave, families must purchase plots in public or private cemeteries.
Military personnel of the {{ill|Empire of Japan|en|Empire of Japan|WD=Q188712|lt=Empire of Japan|1=Empire of Japan}} were often enshrined in Shinto graves separate from their family's ancestral plots. During {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, sunny locations were allocated for the war dead<ref>Hideyoshi Ukai, ''Temple Disappearance,'' Nikkei BP, ISBN 978-4822279172, p.205.</ref>. Gravestones for officers sometimes included their branch (Army or Navy) and final Rank before their names.
== References == {{reflist}}
